# CHAPTER 1

# **CHAPTER 1**

**Introduction**  
**1.1 Rationale of the Study**

The foundational practice of physical therapy is currently undergoing a significant paradigm shift, transitioning from the static evaluation of isolated joints to the "Movement System" model \[cb\]\[cc\]. Traditionally, clinical assessment has relied on the Universal Goniometer (UG), which remains the standard for cost-effectiveness but is inherently restricted to single-joint, static measurements \[bm\]\[f\]\[d\]. This limitation is increasingly pronounced as modern rehabilitation prioritizes dynamic coordination, timing, and functional capacity over isolated flexibility \[ag\]\[am\]. However, a significant technological gap prevents the widespread adoption of dynamic assessment in routine practice \[l\]\[m\]. Current instrumentation forces clinicians to choose between accessible but obsolete static tools or sophisticated laboratory systems that are impractical for clinical use \[n\]\[k\]. 

Interviews with licensed Physical Therapists (PTs) reveal that this technological gap is compounded by severe practical constraints in the field \[cp\]\[cx\]\[cz\]. A primary "pain point" identified by clinicians is the difficulty of accurately identifying the "anatomical fulcrum," particularly in complex cases such as stroke patients \[da\]\[aw\]. This difficulty frequently forces therapists to rely on manual estimation during goniometer placement, introducing significant human error and reducing measurement consistency \[d\]\[e\]\[ao\]. Furthermore, the typical 10-minute assessment window in clinical scenarios leaves little room for the manual alignment and data entry required by traditional tools \[bm\]\[bn\]. Existing advanced systems often fail to provide the efficiency needed for rapid data gathering and automated documentation, further discouraging their adoption in fast-paced clinical workflows \[cp\]\[cq\].

Existing electrogoniometers attempt to bridge this gap but often fall short due to mechanical wear or limited scalability \[b\]\[ar\]\[as\]\[cd\]. Many commercial options are designed as standalone, single-joint instruments that cannot capture the synchronized movement of an entire limb \[b\]\[i\]\[g\]. To address these limitations, this study proposes the development of a modular, multi-joint electrogoniometer framework specifically engineered for a single limb (e.g., Shoulder-Elbow-Wrist or Hip-Knee-Ankle) \[ce\]\[cf\]\[cg\]. By utilizing absolute magnetic encoders (MT6701), the system eliminates the "zero-position" loss and drift issues inherent in IMUs \[ch\]\[ci\]\[cj\]. The body-attached architecture is designed to eliminate the need for manual fulcrum alignment, directly addressing the reliability concerns raised by practicing clinicians \[ct\]\[bl\]\[bh\].

This research is rationalized by the urgent need for a "middle-ground" instrument—one that delivers the dynamic data acquisition capabilities of a motion capture lab while retaining the clinical feasibility and affordability of a traditional goniometer \[bq\]\[br\]\[bs\]. By utilizing cost-effective, Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) components like the ESP32-C3, the study aims to ensure that high-fidelity motion analysis remains financially accessible \[cu\]\[cv\]\[cw\]. Furthermore, the decision to validate the system using an Automated Mechanical Goniometry Rig, rather than human subjects, ensures that the technical feasibility and sensor accuracy are quantified in a controlled environment, isolating electronic performance from human-derived variables such as muscle tremors or skin movement \[aw\]\[aj\]\[ak\]. This study seeks to demonstrate that a reliable, multi-joint wearable is a viable solution for modernizing clinical rehabilitation and providing therapists with the rapid, objective data necessary for effective patient care \[cv\]\[cq\]\[cz\].

**1.2 Statement of the Problem**  
	The current clinical standard for evaluating joint Range of Motion (ROM), the Universal Goniometer (UG), is inherently restricted to static, single-joint assessments. This limitation is increasingly problematic as modern physical therapy shifts toward dynamic movement analysis and the "Movement System" model, which prioritizes coordinated functional capacity and timing over isolated static flexibility \[cb\]\[u\]\[al\].   
Clinical interviews with licensed physical therapists (PTs) reveal critical "pain points" that hinder effective assessment in real-world scenarios \[cp\]\[da\]. One of the challenges identified is the difficulty of accurately identifying the "anatomical fulcrum," particularly in complex cases such as stroke patients \[d\]\[af\]\[bn\]. This difficulty frequently forces clinicians to rely on manual estimation during goniometer positioning, which introduces significant measurement errors and compromises the reliability of the collected data \[d\]\[bn\]\[ao\]. 

Furthermore, clinicians operate within strict time constraints, typically facing a 10-minute assessment window in clinical scenarios. Existing tools often fail to provide the efficiency required for rapid data gathering and automated documentation within this timeframe, as manual alignment and note-taking further strain clinical workflows \[bn\]\[bx\]\[cp\]. While technological alternatives exist, they remain largely impractical for routine clinical adoption \[cq\]\[cr\].

To address these clinical gaps, this study proposes an electro-goniometer with multi-joint movement support, optimized for clinical workflow. By utilizing a body-attached sensor architecture, the proposed solution eliminates the need for PTs to manually align a fulcrum, thereby directly increasing measurement reliability and consistency across different users and patients \[b\]\[d\]\[g\]. This scalable architecture aims to provide a clinically feasible alternative that delivers immediate, accurate readings and streamlined data processing \[bt\]\[bu\]\[bz\], addressing the urgent need for tools that are fast to attach, instantaneous in data saving, and capable of rapid document generation \[cp\]\[cz\]\[cr\]. 

**1.3 Objectives of the Study**  
	This study aims to establish a practical framework for improving joint Range of Motion (ROM) assessment using a body-attached electro-goniometer with multi-joint movement support. Specifically, the researchers aim to:

1\. Develop a body-attached electro-goniometer system that eliminates the need for manual fulcrum alignment, thereby increasing measurement reliability and consistency.  
2\. Implement real-time data collection and automated documentation to streamline clinical workflows and reduce assessment time.  
3\. Evaluate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed electro-goniometer compared to the traditional Universal Goniometer in terms of measurement accuracy and efficiency.

**1.4 Significance of the Study**  
This study aims to address the limitations of the traditional Universal Goniometer in evaluating joint Range of Motion (ROM) by proposing an electro-goniometer with multi-joint movement support, optimized for clinical workflow. The device improves measurement accuracy by eliminating the need for manual fulcrum alignment, reduces human error, and allows for real-time data collection and automated documentation, making assessments faster and more reliable. This innovation benefits physical therapists by streamlining evaluations within limited appointment times, supports patients through precise and consistent assessments for better rehabilitation planning, and contributes to rehabilitation technology by offering a practical, clinically feasible alternative to static, manual measurement tools.  
**1.5 Scope and Delimitations**  
**1.5.1 Scope**  
	This study focuses on the design, development, and evaluation of a body-attached electro-goniometer with multi-joint movement support for clinical joint Range of Motion (ROM) assessment. The scope includes the selection and integration of cost-efficient, clinically accessible hardware using Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) components, specifically the ESP32-C3 microcontroller and MT6701 magnetic encoders. The study also covers real-time data acquisition, automated documentation, and performance evaluation of the system in terms of measurement accuracy, reliability, efficiency, and user satisfaction compared to the traditional Universal Goniometer.  
**1.5.2 Delimitations**  
	The study is delimited to short-duration clinical assessments and selected functional movements consistent with typical physical therapy workflows. It does not include long-term patient monitoring, advanced biomechanical motion analysis, or rehabilitation outcome evaluation. The system evaluation is limited to controlled or simulated clinical environments, and findings may not be generalizable to all patient populations or clinical settings. Additionally, the study does not address mass production, regulatory approval, or cost–benefit analysis beyond the use of affordable COTS components.

**1.6 Definition of Terms**  
**Electro-goniometer-**  A device attached to the body that measures joint angles continuously during movement.  
**Kinovea-** Software that analyzes movement from videos, measuring angles and distances of joints.

**Range of motion-** The total movement possible at a joint, in degrees, including both active and passive motion.

**Soft Tissue Artifact (STA)-** Errors in measurement caused by the skin and muscles moving over the bones during motion.  
**Universal Goniometer (UG):** The current clinical gold standard for evaluating joint Range of Motion (ROM), consisting of a protractor-like device with two movable arms used for static, single-joint assessment

# Chapter 2

# **CHAPTER 2**

**REVIEW OF RELATED LITERATURE**

## **2.1 Paradigm Shift in Physical Therapy Instrumentation**

Traditionally, physical therapy relied on Range of Motion (ROM) assessment using tools like the universal goniometer to evaluate isolated joint mobility \[3\], \[41\], \[51\]. While simple and cost-effective, this approach is not capable of capturing the complexity of functional movement, including coordination, timing, and stability \[7\], \[51\].

The American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) and other organizations such as the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) have driven a shift toward evaluating the Movement System through frameworks like the 4-Element Movement System Model (Motion, Force, Motor Control, Energy), emphasizing dynamic, integrated assessments over isolated joint measures \[7\] \- \[9\], \[52\], . This approach allows clinicians to create comprehensive movement profiles that better predict injury risk and guide rehabilitation \[51\].

Technological advances support this shift. Inertial measurement units (IMUs) and optical motion capture systems, including markerless approaches, now enable objective measurement of multi-joint kinematics and functional tasks, overcoming limitations of manual goniometry \[13\], \[14\], \[17\], \[46\]. However, several challenges limit their clinical implementation. These include sensor drift, soft-tissue artifacts that can distort joint kinematics, and the need for compliance with regulatory frameworks such as the FDA and EU MDR \[17\], \[53\], \[54\]. Additionally, the practical deployment of these technologies is constrained by spatial requirements, high acquisition and maintenance costs, and the specialized technical expertise required for operation and data interpretation, all of which hinder widespread adoption in routine clinical practice \[11\], \[16\].

Currently, manual ROM measurement remains the industry standard, but the field is moving toward technology-assisted movement analysis, integrating ROM as one element of a broader, function-focused evaluation \[8\], \[52\]. This transition promises more accurate, standardized, and clinically relevant assessments, though widespread adoption is still in progress \[8\], \[12\], \[51\].

### **2.2 The Universal Goniometer (UG)**

The Universal Goniometer (UG) is the gold standard for assessing joint range of motion (ROM) in both clinical and research settings. Accurate measurement of ROM is essential for diagnosing musculoskeletal disorders, evaluating rehabilitation progress, and monitoring the outcomes of therapeutic interventions \[40\], \[58\]–\[60\]. The UG consists of a protractor-like device with two movable arms, which allows clinicians to quantify joint angles with relative precision \[58\], \[59\]. Its simplicity, portability, and affordability have made it the most commonly used instrument in physical therapy, orthopedics, sports medicine, and rehabilitation programs \[3\].

The continued relevance of the UG is reflected in its role as a reference standard for validating newer measurement tools, such as digital goniometers and motion capture systems \[1\]\[56\]\[61\]–\[67\]. While these modern instruments offer advantages in dynamic assessment and data recording, the UG remains widely adopted due to its minimal equipment requirements and ease of use in various clinical environments \[64\]. Its accessibility allows clinicians to conduct objective assessments of joint mobility, which are critical for planning and evaluating treatment strategies.

Studies have reported that the UG demonstrates generally high reliability, particularly in terms of intra-rater reliability, where repeated measurements by the same examiner are consistent \[43\]\[63\]. However, inter-rater reliability, or agreement between different examiners, tends to be lower due to variations in landmark identification and measurement technique \[44\]\[68\]. An  examiner experience significantly influences measurement consistency, emphasizing the importance of proper training and adherence to standardized protocols when using the UG \[42\], \[64\]. In addition, measurement procedures differ across protocols and regions, such as the Korean Range of Motion Standard Protocol (KRSP), which standardizes joint positioning, stabilization, and measurement sequence to reduce variability \[55\], \[61\].  (See 2.2.1.1)

Despite its widespread use, the UG has several limitations. Human error is inherent in manually aligning the device and reading angles, which may affect measurement accuracy \[65\], \[68\]. In addition, the UG is unable to capture dynamic or functional joint movements, restricting its utility in situations that require real-time motion analysis \[2\]. The process can also be time-consuming, particularly when multiple joints require evaluation or repeated trials are necessary \[68\]. These limitations have motivated research into digital goniometers and motion capture technologies that aim to reduce examiner-dependent variability, improve efficiency, and enable dynamic assessment \[55\], \[57\].

Overall, the Universal Goniometer remains the gold standard and reliable tool for joint ROM assessment. Its simplicity, accessibility, and generally high measurement consistency make it a cornerstone in musculoskeletal evaluation, although human factors and limitations in dynamic assessment continue to drive the development of complementary digital measurement tools \[64\], \[65\].

### **2.3 Limitations of Manual Goniometric Assessment** 	The Universal Goniometer (UG) remains a widely used tool for assessing joint Range of Motion (ROM) due to its affordability and ease of use; however, empirical studies have highlighted several limitations that affect its reliability and clinical applicability. One significant source of error is the difficulty in accurately identifying anatomical landmarks and aligning the fulcrum, particularly in patients with altered musculoskeletal structures or neurological conditions, such as post-stroke individuals \[1\]\[42\]\[64\]. This often necessitates visual estimation or palpation by the clinician, which increases measurement variability and reduces consistency across assessments \[1\]\[64\]\[69\]. Additionally, both inter-rater and intra-rater variability have been documented, indicating that ROM measurements can differ depending on the examiner’s experience, technique, and interpretation of joint endpoints \[64\]\[74\].

### Time constraints in clinical practice further limit the effectiveness of manual goniometry. Physical therapists typically operate under brief assessment periods, often around 10 minutes per patient, during which precise alignment, repeated measurements, and manual documentation can be difficult to achieve consistently \[64\]\[74\]\[12\]. Consequently, assessment procedures may be simplified or rushed, potentially affecting data quality and clinical decision-making \[12\]\[48\]. Moreover, manual goniometry is restricted to static, single-joint measurements, which limits its applicability in evaluating dynamic or multi-joint movements emphasized in contemporary physical therapy approaches such as the Movement System model \[80\]\[7\]. These documented limitations underscore the need for assessment tools that provide accurate, reliable, and efficient ROM measurements, forming the rationale for the development of body-attached electro-goniometer systems in clinical practice.

**2.4 Technological Advances in ROM Measurement**  
	Despite the shift toward functional assessment, the precise measurement of joint angles remains a fundamental skill for clinicians and physical therapists \[2\], \[3\]. The tools for this task have evolved from simple mechanical protractors to complex digital systems, each with distinct reliability profiles and error sources \[55\]–\[57\].

### **2.4.1 Electrogoniometers**

Electrogoniometers have emerged as a significant advancement over traditional mechanical goniometers by offering more precise and continuous tracking of joint Range of Motion (ROM) \[22\]\[26\]\[73\]. Early devices primarily relied on potentiometers to measure angular displacement, providing real-time data for clinical and research applications \[73\]. While these potentiometer-based electrogoniometers improved measurement accuracy compared to manual goniometry, they were often bulky, rigid, and limited to single-plane movements, constraining their applicability in dynamic or multi-joint assessments \[73\]\[30\]. Subsequent developments introduced flexible strain gauges and textile-based sensors, enabling wearable designs that conform to the body, reduce interference with natural movement, and allow monitoring during dynamic activities \[22\]\[57\]\[71\]\[74\]. These advancements have facilitated multi-axis measurements, which are particularly advantageous for joints such as the wrist, knee, and elbow \[22\]\[27\]\[49\]\[75\].

Despite these technological improvements, electrogoniometers still face notable limitations. Many systems remain sensitive to placement errors, sensor drift, and soft-tissue artifacts, which can compromise measurement reliability during prolonged or high-speed motion \[105\]\[54\]\[17\]. Additionally, setup complexity, calibration requirements, and cost continue to restrict their widespread adoption in routine clinical practice \[25\]\[57\]\[12\]. Nevertheless, the evolution of electrogoniometer technology demonstrates a clear trend toward wearable, lightweight, and multi-joint systems capable of dynamic motion tracking, highlighting both their potential to enhance clinical assessments and the persistent challenges that must be addressed for practical implementation \[22\]\[75\]\[76\].

**2.4.2 Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)**

Recent advances in wearable technology have positioned Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) as versatile tools for assessing joint Range of Motion (ROM), capable of capturing continuous, multi-planar movements without the need for rigid optical systems or manual alignment \[19\]\[48\]. By combining accelerometers, gyroscopes, and sometimes magnetometers, IMUs deliver real-time data on joint angles and dynamic motion patterns, supporting both clinical and research applications \[20\]\[18\]. Improvements in sensor miniaturization, wearability, and algorithmic processing now allow IMUs to track complex, functional movements over extended periods \[50\]. However, their measurement accuracy can be influenced by soft-tissue artifacts, sensor misplacement, and magnetic interference, which may introduce errors \[54\]\[17\]. Moreover, effective clinical adoption requires careful calibration, data processing expertise, and integration into existing workflows \[12\]\[9\]. Despite these challenges, IMUs offer significant advantages for dynamic and portable joint assessment, provided their limitations are acknowledged and managed in rehabilitation and physical therapy settings \[18\]\[54\]\[50\].

### **2.2.3 Inclinometers and Gravity-Based Sensors**

### Inclinometers, also known as tilt sensors or clinometers, use gravity as a constant reference vector to determine the orientation of a body segment, eliminating the need to align a stationary arm with a proximal segment and thus reducing alignment errors \[64\]. Their portability, ease of use, and relatively low cost make them suitable for both clinical and field assessments of joint range of motion (ROM) \[3\], \[64\]. Unlike universal goniometers, inclinometers do not require the examiner to visually estimate angles or maintain strict anatomical alignment, which minimizes inter-rater and intra-rater variability \[64\], \[55\].

	Research indicates that inclinometers demonstrate moderate to high reliability for joint angle measurements \[64\]. Inter-rater reliability tends to be higher for active movements, while passive lower extremity joint measurements may show lower consistency across examiners \[64\]. Systematic reviews suggest that instrumented measurements improve consistency compared to visual estimation, although clinically significant errors can still occur \[64\], \[69\]. In terms of validity, inclinometer measurements generally show strong agreement with standard goniometers and other reference methods, supporting their use for both clinical evaluation and functional assessment \[64\].

### **2.4.4 Smartphone Applications**

The ubiquity of smartphones has introduced a new era of "pocket goniometry." These apps utilize the phone's built-in IMU sensors \[78\], \[79\]. Early reviews highlighted the rapid emergence of smartphone-based goniometric tools and their growing use in rehabilitation settings, noting that many apps demonstrated measurement capabilities comparable to conventional instruments \[78\]. Subsequent studies further reported acceptable reliability and concurrent validity when smartphone applications were compared with standard goniometers across both experienced and novice clinicians \[105\]. Recent evaluations have continued to support the clinical utility of these applications, showing that smartphone-based measurements can achieve intrarater and interrater reliability similar to digital inclinometers and universal goniometers in post-operative populations \[55\]. Systematic evidence also indicates that clinically accessible smartphone apps have matured into viable alternatives for range-of-motion assessment, provided that measurement protocols are standardized and sensor alignment is carefully controlled \[79\].

A systematic review of 25 studies found that smartphone apps demonstrated adequate reliability and validity for over 50% of ROM tests across all joints, providing sufficient evidence to support their use as alternatives to universal goniometers. However, the level of evidence was found to be weaker for absolute measures, such as mean difference and limits of agreement, compared to relative measures like correlation. This suggests that while these apps are excellent for tracking changes over time (relative reliability), their absolute accuracy in degrees compared to a gold standard (validity) may vary \[79\].

## **2.5 Technologies in Clinical Movement Assessment**

	Clinical movement assessment now uses a range of technologies to measure joint angles and motion accurately. From traditional goniometers to wearable sensors and real-time data systems, each tool has distinct advantages and limitations. The following sections explore these technologies, focusing on body-attached sensors and automated data collection for improved accuracy and clinical efficiency.

## **2.5.1 Body-Attached Sensor Systems and Feasibility**

	Recent advancements in rehabilitation technology have highlighted the potential of body-attached sensor systems for improving clinical movement assessment \[75\], \[36\]. By directly mounting sensors—such as inertial measurement units (IMUs), flexible electrogoniometers, and strain gauges—on the limbs, these systems overcome challenges inherent in traditional methods, including manual alignment errors, repeated calibration, and inter-rater variability \[19\], \[25\], \[28\]. These devices offer continuous, real-time tracking of multi-planar joint movements, providing richer and more objective datasets than conventional goniometry or optical motion capture systems \[3\], \[15\], \[29\].

The clinical appeal of these systems lies in their portability and practicality \[50\]. Lightweight body-attached sensors allow patients to perform exercises or daily activities naturally, enabling clinicians to collect longitudinal data on range of motion and movement quality \[76\], \[6\]. Evidence suggests that when integrated effectively, these systems enhance measurement reliability and reproducibility, supporting data-driven treatment adjustments and monitoring of rehabilitation progress \[105\], \[75\].

However, the widespread clinical feasibility of these systems is currently constrained by significant technical and operational challenges. Measurement accuracy is frequently compromised by soft-tissue artifacts, sensor misplacement, and magnetic interference, which can introduce errors exceeding acceptable clinical thresholds \[54\], \[21\], \[17\]. Addressing these issues requires strict calibration protocols and signal processing procedures, yet the complexity of setup and data interpretation often demands specialized expertise not standard in routine clinical practice \[35\], \[10\]. Furthermore, successful implementation relies on patient adherence; long-term wear comfort and usability remain key factors affecting data completeness in unsupervised rehabilitation settings \[12\]. Consequently, while feasible, the transition of these devices from research tools to clinical staples depends on resolving usability, calibration, and clinician-training barriers \[12\], \[10\].

## **2.5.2 Real-Time Data Acquisition and Automated Clinical Documentation**

## Advancements in real-time data acquisition and automated clinical documentation have increasingly enhanced the practice of physical therapy by providing continuous, objective monitoring of patient movement and joint Range of Motion (ROM) \[12\]\[48\]. Modern systems, including wearable sensors, Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs), and pressure-sensitive devices, allow for the capture of multi-planar movement patterns during both clinical and functional tasks, offering higher temporal and spatial resolution compared to traditional goniometry and manual recording \[50\]\[75\]. Studies demonstrate that automated data collection reduces inter- and intra-rater variability by up to 30–40% in ROM measurements, while enabling clinicians to identify compensatory movement patterns that may not be apparent through observation alone \[105\]\[75\]. Furthermore, automated documentation systems can generate standardized progress reports, graphical analyses, and trend visualizations directly from sensor data, significantly decreasing administrative time and allowing therapists to devote more time to patient care \[106\]. Evidence also shows that integration of these systems into rehabilitation programs can improve patient adherence, as digital feedback and real-time progress visualization have been associated with a 15–20% increase in exercise compliance in outpatient settings \[50\]\[9\].

## Despite these advantages, several limitations constrain their widespread clinical adoption. High acquisition costs, ongoing maintenance, and the need for clinician training in device calibration, data processing, and software interpretation can pose barriers to implementation \[12\]\[107\]\[48\]. Measurement accuracy may also be compromised by soft-tissue artifacts, sensor misplacement, or connectivity issues, introducing potential errors that require careful calibration and verification \[54\]\[17\]\[18\]. Compatibility with existing electronic health record systems remains an additional challenge, often necessitating custom integration solutions \[12\]\[9\]. Nonetheless, evidence indicates that when these barriers are addressed, real-time data acquisition and automated documentation can enhance clinical efficiency, provide objective metrics for functional assessment, support data-driven treatment adjustments, and contribute to more personalized rehabilitation programs \[50\]\[48\].

## **2.6 Current Motion Analysis Instrumentations**

While wearable sensors provide convenient and portable means to capture joint movement, optical analysis remains the gold standard for evaluating complex motions like gait \[48\], \[75\], \[76\]. Wearables such as electrogoniometers, IMUs, and strain sensors offer practical monitoring but are limited by issues like sensor drift, soft tissue artifacts, and calibration errors \[17\], \[18\], \[22\], \[49\], \[54\].

Motion analysis now splits into two main optical approaches: accessible 2D video analysis, using software like Kinovea, which allows reliable joint angle and spatiotemporal measurements \[45\]–\[47\], \[87\],  and high-fidelity 3D motion capture systems that provide precise three-dimensional tracking of limb and body movements \[11\], \[13\], \[14\], \[16\]. Combining wearable and optical methods leverages the strengths of both portability and accuracy, enhancing gait analysis, rehabilitation, and functional assessment \[8\], \[81\], \[85\].

### **2.6.1 3D Motion Capture: The Gold Standard and Its Barriers**

Marker-based 3D motion capture (e.g., Vicon, Qualisys) uses multiple infrared cameras to track reflective markers placed on anatomical landmarks. It provides sub-millimeter precision and is considered the benchmark for validating all other systems. However, the barriers to clinical adoption are high. These systems are expensive ($10k+), require a dedicated laboratory space, and demand significant time for setup and post-processing. Furthermore, the accuracy is contingent on the clinician's ability to place markers on the correct anatomical landmarks, with "marker placement error" being a documented source of variability \[11\], \[54\]. Markerless motion capture, which uses computer vision to identify joints without markers, is emerging to address setup time and marker artifact issues. Reviews indicate that while temporospatial parameters such as speed and step length are accurate, joint angle calculations—especially for the shoulder—are not yet equivalent to marker-based systems \[16\], \[86\].

### **2.6.2 2D Video Analysis: Kinovea**

Kinovea is a free, open-source 2D motion analysis software widely used in clinical and sports settings. It allows clinicians to measure angles and times from standard video recordings. Multiple studies have validated Kinovea against 3D systems. Kinovea demonstrates excellent reliability (ICC \> 0.90) for measuring sagittal plane parameters such as flexion and extension during gait and functional tasks \[45\], \[87\]. When compared to gold-standard optical systems like Vicon, OptiTrack, and Qualisys, its validity remains strong in the sagittal plane, showing high correlation for hip and knee flexion angles \[46\], \[47\], \[87\]. However, validity decreases notably in the coronal (abduction) and transverse (rotation) planes because a single-camera setup cannot capture depth or out-of-plane motion accurately \[46\]. In the clinical 2D market, Kinovea competes with commercial software like Dartfish and mobile applications such as Hudl Technique; while these alternatives offer similar utility for basic angular measurement, Kinovea is often favored in research for its open-access transparency and advanced trajectory tracking capabilities \[88\], \[89\].

#### **2.6.2.1 Parallax Error and Perspective**

The critical limitation of 2D analysis is parallax error, a geometric distortion that occurs when the movement plane is not perpendicular to the camera axis, causing relative distances and angles to shift based on the viewer's line of sight \[90\], \[91\]. Research consistently confirms that a 90° recording angle (perpendicular to the subject) is the optimal standard for minimizing this artifact \[47\], \[92\]. However, studies indicate that 2D systems retain some utility even at oblique angles, provided the specific joint limitations are understood. For instance, a 2025 study on canine gait demonstrated that at a 45° recording angle, Kinovea could still accurately measure the carpus and hip (difference \< 5° relative to 3D motion capture), whereas other joints showed significantly higher errors \[92\]. Similarly, at 135°, accuracy varied considerably by joint, with only the elbow and hip retaining acceptable validity \[92\]. These findings highlight that while 2D analysis is robust, it is highly sensitive to camera placement, and deviations from the perpendicular axis can result in velocity errors up to 2.5 times higher than those observed at 90° \[91\].

#### **2.6.2.2 Best Practices in 2D Motion Analysis** 

Accurate 2D motion analysis requires careful control of multiple factors to ensure reliable measurements. The camera should be mounted on a stable tripod at the height of the joint being analyzed and positioned strictly perpendicular to the plane of motion to reduce parallax and perspective errors  \[45\], \[93\]. Maintaining a standardized distance, typically between 1.5 m and 5 m depending on the camera lens, helps reduce distortion caused by lens curvature and ensures consistent scaling across trials \[47\], \[87\]. While markerless software can track joint motion using pixel-based detection, the placement of physical markers on anatomical landmarks, such as the greater trochanter or lateral epicondyle, significantly improves the reliability and accuracy of the tracking algorithm \[13\], \[16\], \[94\]. Additionally, ensuring consistent lighting conditions, minimizing background clutter, and using high-resolution cameras can further enhance the precision of joint angle measurements \[45\], \[87\]. Calibration with a reference object or grid prior to data collection is also recommended to correct for any residual lens or perspective distortion, thereby improving the overall validity of the analysis \[45\], \[47\], \[93\].

**2.7 Soft Tissue Artifact (STA)**  
	The most significant barrier to accurate non-invasive measurement is Soft Tissue Artifact (STA). This is the error caused by the movement of skin, muscle, and fat relative to the underlying bone. STA can introduce errors of up to 20–30 mm in position or 10–20° in joint angle, often exceeding the clinical thresholds for acceptable accuracy \[54\]. In high-impact activities like running, the inertia of the soft tissue causes markers to oscillate independent of the skeleton, creating false movement data that mimics rigid body motion \[21\]. Research indicates that STA is often a larger source of error than marker placement or instrument precision; for example, in shoulder kinematics, STA was found to contribute to 27% of kinetic variability, compared to only 11% for marker offset \[53\]. To mitigate this, researchers employ Multi-Body Optimization (MBO) algorithms that enforce rigid body constraints on the data, mathematically forcing the disjointed marker trajectories to conform to a viable skeletal model \[103\]. In wearable design, mitigating STA requires hardware-level solutions: minimizing the mass of the sensor to reduce inertial wobble and prioritizing strap placement on bony areas with minimal muscle coverage, such as the tibial shank rather than the calf muscle \[21\].

## **2.8. Hardware and Sensor Technology Engineering**

The transition from analog to digital physical therapy requires a deep understanding of the underlying sensor physics. The accuracy of any digital goniometer or motion capture system is dictated by the limitations of its hardware components. Accelerometers are susceptible to noise during rapid movement because they cannot distinguish gravitational acceleration from limb acceleration, while gyroscopes accumulate angular drift over time, leading to progressively inaccurate estimates unless corrected by sensor fusion algorithms \[17\]–\[19\], \[49\]. Magnetometers, meanwhile, are vulnerable to magnetic distortion from nearby ferromagnetic objects, electrical equipment, or environmental interference, causing heading errors that propagate through orientation calculations ,issues consistently reported in IMU-based biomechanics research \[17\], \[18\], \[49\], and analogous concerns noted in electrogoniometer signal contamination \[24\], \[27\]. 

Even electrogoniometers, despite their higher precision, are limited by mechanical alignment, joint axis variability, and susceptibility to crosstalk between orthogonal planes of movement \[22\], \[26\], \[27\], \[74\]. These hardware-level constraints illustrate that digital measurement tools, while powerful, remain bounded by the physical behavior of their sensors and the fidelity of the systems that integrate them \[57\], \[75\],  \[76\].

### **2.8.1 Rotary Encoders vs. Potentiometers**

For applications requiring robust and direct angle measurement—such as robotic exoskeletons or instrumented rehabilitation machines—engineers must choose between potentiometers and rotary encoders. Potentiometers are simple and cost-effective but may suffer from drift and mechanical wear \[26\], \[30\], \[73\]. Rotary encoders, on the other hand, provide higher resolution, repeatability, and reliability, making them better suited for precise rehabilitation and robotic systems \[31\] \- \[33\], \[80\]. The choice depends on accuracy, durability, and the specific demands of the application \[81\], \[82\].

#### **2.8.1.1 Potentiometers**

Potentiometric goniometers, or potentiometer-based angle sensors, measure angular displacement by converting rotational motion into changes in electrical resistance through a sliding wiper mechanism. Early biomechanical studies showed that potentiometric goniometers were widely used because they are simple, low-cost, and easy to implement in basic electrogoniometric systems \[26\], \[73\]. However, research has also highlighted several drawbacks, such as mechanical wear from continuous contact between the wiper and resistive track, which compromises long-term reliability and accuracy \[26\], \[49\]. Their rigid structure can interfere with natural joint motion, making them less suitable for capturing complex or multi-plane movements compared to more flexible electrogoniometers \[26\], \[30\]. Additional issues such as alignment sensitivity, hysteresis, and reduced performance during dynamic tasks further limit their effectiveness for precise biomechanical assessment \[30\], \[73\].   

#### **2.8.1.2 Rotary Encoders**

	Rotary encoders are extensively used in precision measurement and motion control due to their capability to provide accurate angular position feedback. Calibration of rotary encoders is critical to ensure measurement accuracy, particularly when interfacing with different systems \[31\]. A study demonstrated the calibration of rotary encoders with various interfaces using a dynamic goniometer, emphasizing the importance of precise alignment and measurement reliability \[31\]. Similarly, a precision goniometer mechanism designed for direct angle measurement with rotary encoders improved resolution and mechanical stability in practical applications \[32\]. A universal dynamic goniometer applicable to multiple rotary encoder types provides a standardized approach for dynamic angular measurement \[33\]. Rotary encoders are also utilized for monitoring the operational health of rotating machinery, illustrating that accurate angular measurements can support predictive maintenance and fault detection \[34\]. Collectively, these studies highlight the integral role of rotary encoders in both precise measurement and industrial monitoring, with dynamic goniometers serving as essential tools for calibration and performance evaluation.  
Encoders are devices that measure angular position by generating digital signals, classified as incremental or absolute. Incremental encoders output pulses and provide high precision but lose position data when power is lost, requiring a homing procedure \[32\], \[33\]. Absolute encoders assign a unique digital code to each position, retaining orientation through power cycles \[34\]. Optical encoders use light passing through a slotted disc, offering high accuracy but being sensitive to dust, while magnetic encoders employ a magnet and Hall-effect sensor, providing robustness and suitability for wearable biomedical applications \[19\], \[12\]. These encoders are commonly integrated into wearable goniometers and motion capture systems for accurate joint kinematics measurement \[22\], \[57\], \[71\].a  
**Table 2.1: Comparative Analysis of Sensor Technologies for Goniometry**

| Feature | Potentiometer | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Magnetic Encoder | IMU (9-axis) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Output | Analog Voltage | Digital Pulses | Digital Word/Code | Orientation Quaternion |
| Durability | Low (Contact wear) | High (Non-contact) | High (Non-contact) | High (Solid State) |
| Drift | None | None | None | High (Gyroscopic) |
| Interference | Electrical Noise | None | Magnetic Fields | Magnetic & Vibration |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Low to Medium |
| Best Use | Simple levers, low-cost tools | Motor speed control | Robotic joints, Exoskeletons | Wearable motion tracking |

### **2.9 Bio-Joint Misalignment and Self-Aligning Mechanisms**

#### **2.9.1 Joint Misalignment**

Human joints are rarely simple hinge (pin) joints. The knee, for example, exhibits a complex combination of rolling and sliding, causing the instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) to follow a J-curve trajectory \[54\], \[80\]. When a rigid exoskeleton or goniometer with a fixed axis is strapped to the leg, the discrepancy between the device’s fixed pivot and the knee’s moving ICR generates parasitic forces. These forces can cause the device to slide along the limb, a phenomenon known as “pistoning,” or to exert shear forces on the skin and underlying joints. Such misalignment may transmit spurious torques of considerable magnitude to the user, potentially resulting in discomfort, skin breakdown, or even joint injury \[80\], \[96\].

#### **2.9.2 Self-Aligning Mechanisms**

Mechanical alignment in knee exoskeletons is critical to reduce unwanted interaction forces and torques. Parallelogram linkages have been employed to address this issue, using a double parallelogram structure that permits rotation while providing translational freedom, allowing the mechanism to “float” and track the joint’s changing center of rotation \[97\], \[98\]. Complementing this approach, Prismatic-Revolute-Revolute (PRR) chains incorporate passive sliding and additional rotational joints, enabling the device to adjust its length dynamically as the knee flexes. Research on a PRR-configured knee exoskeleton demonstrated its effectiveness in minimizing misalignment, with spurious torques reduced to below 0.5 Nm and interaction forces kept under 5 N during sit-to-stand movements \[80\].

# Chapter 3

#  **CHAPTER 3**

**METHODOLOGY**

## **3.1 Conceptual Framework**

This study employs an Input-Process-Output (IPO) model to structure the development and validation of the modular electrogoniometer. This framework is anchored on the transition from traditional, manual measurement techniques to an automated, clinician-centric digital workflow.

**Table 3.1 IPO Model**

| Input | Process | Output |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Clinical Constraints *(Assessment time, Accuracy, Repeatability)* Functional Requirements *(Simultaneous measurement)* Hardware and Software Design Requirements *(Setup, Reliability)* Hardware Selection (Cost-effective, widely available)  | System Development *(Hardware assembly, Firmware deployment)* Software Implementation *(Offset correction, Automated feedback)* Controlled Testing *(Static accuracy & Gait-specific simulation)* Accuracy Validation Protocols *(Static vs. UG, Dynamic vs. Kinovea)*Workflow Simulation (Timed setup-to-report protocol) | Functional Prototype *(Versatile, modular multi-joint electrogoniometer)* Feasibility Analysis *(Technical validity, Workflow integration)* Performance Metrics *(Accuracy, Documentation speed)* Efficiency Data *(Setup time, Processing latency)* |
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |

### **3.1.1 Input**

The input phase defines the foundational requirements for a clinician-centric measurement tool. It incorporates Clinical Constraints, such as the ten-minute assessment window and the need for repeatable measurements in stroke rehabilitation. Technical Constraints address the limitations of the manual Universal Goniometer and the synchronization requirements for multi-joint sensing. Design Requirements focus on modularity and the automation of documentation, with the multi-joint scope limited to lower-extremity gait analysis. Hardware Selection utilizes cost-effective Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) components and a wired sensor linkage to prioritize signal integrity over wireless complexity.

### **3.1.2 Process**

The process phase outlines the iterative development and validation of the system. System Development involves the physical assembly of the modular device and firmware deployment for synchronized data capture. Software Implementation includes the development of the web-based interface and clinical algorithms for automated feedback and post-processing offset correction. Controlled Testing utilizes a mechanical rig to evaluate static accuracy and simulate lower-extremity movement patterns. Finally, Validation Protocols compare the device against the UG and Kinovea, followed by a workflow simulation in a lab environment to verify the setup-to-report efficiency.

### **3.1.3 Output**

The output stage provides the final deliverables and performance data of the research. The Functional Prototype represents a modular, versatile electrogoniometer validated for clinical use. The Feasibility Analysis assesses the technical validity and the system's potential for seamless integration into the PT workflow. Performance Metrics document the system’s accuracy and the speed of its automated reporting features. The Clinical Data Suite serves as the digital output for practitioners, providing automated PDF reports and replayable session logs to facilitate longitudinal progress tracking.

## **3.2 Research Workflow**

This study follows a systematic research design structured around the Input-Process-Output (IPO) model established in the conceptual framework. The methodology is executed through a linear, three-phase workflow that progresses from modular integration to empirical validation, ensuring that the fundamental electronic architecture is stabilized before mechanical variables are introduced.

[![][image2]](https://www.plantuml.com/plantuml/img/RLF1RXGn3BtdAwmUgbq9a6XlinBgYAMb4j1gjvGB5vFnxbZD927nT3iW_fqaHKmgRGvHDBQVttiUNqJ18EDWL7nWwp7W08OjoUG9N93ULO6ETZWQkQCdQGtlxK1MGAUBWX8MGt3-vWms50c3xk6R2myxumvAbIPA9PIKnix2oMsFaU1j0zif2WrmdK3s0Klm52uv30SC16lonasvpmdyLg0WdN85yeHIdlaq7p5qfQ82MitX2l2HZFCPiGflUE-26oDyjInmHr7OxbTL8pZqdEGjje8_sB1CS4-1TwnBeoLmXCKNjomLhzz1JZEqKd-EQ3vhuBsp4fmnrC5dqZtQX6FW7Wrt1Qze1LNGcYt9wA4TnGqeBokkd6Kta8G9aeBLx4xqYUCiBz4Hrj36I37EqaiMu1xPphdhoU9m99biLy4-KrkZ8BJwvyYnIFnk5wtMOq0zlO9FYQtDZ-raDMIMhEFok2Vd3LmIHlxdRMlHJ35vUWetNdZWNvKjip5PLPfKTYBra9voYTl1XoUVXfEffqZcM3dJNA7_Z__YErbAlmsbwUl-sTwQqePyseQwu6OKFqe3ruCFxf7ABjm6vrr4KrH6SLvT9D_ITlq5)  
*Figure 3.1. Research Workflow*

The first phase focuses on the physical assembly of the modular hardware architecture. This stage involves the interconnection of discrete pre-fabricated subsystems, including the ESP32-C3 microcontroller and MT6701 magnetic sensor nodes, into a unified wearable prototype. Firmware development focuses on the internal data collection and processing pipeline for up to four simultaneous measurements for gait analysis. The phase concludes with rigorous unit testing of the sensor protocols and battery management to verify that the Device-Under-Test (DUT) achieves operational stability prior to mechanical mounting.

Following system stabilization, the second phase, Controlled Mechanical Validation, focuses on empirical testing. To ensure accuracy, this phase eliminates human variables—such as skin movement and muscle tremors—by testing the device exclusively on an Automated Mechanical Goniometry Rig. This rig provides a stable, programmable testing environment. The evaluation proceeds in two steps. First, the device undergoes a "Staircase Protocol," where the motor moves to fixed angles and holds its position. This allows researchers to compare the device's static readings directly against a Universal Goniometer. Second, the rig performs continuous, high-speed sweeping motions. This dynamic test measures how well the device tracks movement over time, specifically checking for signal delay (latency) and accuracy errors using the Kinovea video analysis software as a reference.

The final phase focuses on the integration of the web application and a comprehensive feasibility analysis. This stage involves developing the clinical data suite to support automated data gathering and document generation that does not exceed one minute. The workflow incorporates the development of features such as replayable measurement sessions and automated feedback logic that alerts the user if a measurement goes beyond the normative range of motion. Additionally, post-processing features are developed to correct angular offsets caused by the physical placement of the device on the body. This phase concludes with a synthesis of the experimental data to provide a validated design blueprint for a clinically integrable solution.

## **3.3 System Architecture**

[![][image3]](https://www.plantuml.com/plantuml/img/TLHDZzem4BtxLumumI6WBZr526s0PHTAjwZfQWzL3yOPWXV7Zcp3abRyzux3Hw4hE839l1c_UVCczyvpwpU5ObvwXJ2M-SOYTDjtCNp7hNIOGLevZmKaLgoaH-43eFaayrKqbXagwIjuvD93ehcgd7GjnaekrZn7Q0oLr598hc6YFTeb5vGxjyY5XrTSG5AMhGRyOK2VdoyJcF4AxI_ezOwOQThl7uBT4NmrNXfDlBYM1G_dWFonieZjDtS2paTZWfJ6UdX4ZPPxKrTySnGvWkglZ7k-u8vO3gnvTsWX9IZHFX6YOYaw5-wScTo58XETuGwpqQ0pCuAhOyAnwfxj6TCc2tsZzfOh61hwDGgUmzFcGphltaN3xh-M4rTfiR96cush1KdHRac1GtH43279SulvgOknjCKxfmaG8oaksv5dWHjfEe71nZM0Ew1pe7MUI8hQ6KlJjBo2vgkqc9tfB2ql2F8aIvWQgWVDqOQhADdHKAz4gKjyYc6iS4VoT1vsdfxL0ucY-ifhABafMMGR4MRMIRBZwGgyPvS9tHY-QCG3ZIlS2V6PIkgr-Xz2hTuK5NfHrB-qMHoYgH5hz0wQq_JRSujz5BrEkbapflquHEJli2oUZ5kmYqlgz1lk3JtarCDBIMP4zo7jsdunpD7IWf7M_kJLO3YFO6aTFPZbhPj8kf7CfUTALM2F0NIGXyMbeAY4ehzIXw2Al2GVaCWiUC8KuFYMUdC7DgxDK6SGxcNidexq-lWB)  
*Figure 3.2. System Architecture Overview*

The system architecture is established upon a modular, single-controller framework designed to prioritize high-speed, synchronized data acquisition for gait analysis. At its core, the architecture defines a unified "Central Node" housing the primary ESP32-C3 microcontroller, which interfaces with a specialized sensor array via a high-priority wired Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI) bus. To capture comprehensive lower-extremity kinematics, the array utilizes four magnetic sensors: two sensors are dedicated to the hip joint to facilitate dual-axis measurement (flexion/extension and abduction/adduction), while the remaining two monitor the knee and ankle. By consolidating all sensing elements into a single hardware stack, the system eliminates the latency and synchronization issues inherent in wireless device-to-device communication, ensuring that multi-joint movement is captured as a single, temporally-aligned data stream suitable for high-velocity gait cycles.

The software layer integrates a lightweight clinician interface built with the Preact JS framework, ensuring the web application remains responsive even when served directly from the ESP32-C3’s internal memory. To bridge the gap between raw numerical data and clinical observation, the system employs a Three.js rendering engine to provide real-time 3D digital reanimation of the captured movement. This allows clinicians to visually replay gait sessions and identify biomechanical compensations from multiple virtual perspectives. Furthermore, session persistence is handled entirely through browser-based storage (LocalStorage/IndexedDB), which avoids the write-speed bottlenecks of the microcontroller's flash memory and ensures data privacy by keeping patient metrics on the local client device. This synergy between high-speed hardware polling and advanced client-side visualization enables an automated "setup-to-report" workflow that meets the strict 10-minute efficiency window required in professional clinical settings.

### **3.3.1 Core System Configuration**

[![][image4]](https://www.plantuml.com/plantuml/img/LLBVJzim47xtNt6FgRIfQ4zJYu2IheC9feXqAtkQhicbiUhucErGu65_-yvEWEx5aPtlrtsw2n_GXRupok-rVK277TJKO6_2cfx2mkYTxSW6gEGWTuJgs7BLEku8Wklfw8zliUQ3jZjeq7XIAdaeDJlTdfNFFb07bypXTBezcwlPjTL1ez4l15VewWCwKbbPNd-0WW_au1OjxYXccCn7ZIW7DroXWOt-kDAmAA9OeYrdy9MjbbWIz_Vz_vmDRQ4azqXkm1SiZvzF8aWtaEL6nr5pjfQgGFNv149B5hBlF540imtg00sxLuHcErSa8qBsYto2HAje068OT1KTlGwKx02og_NjpVHR2Otq1b-U9AAL8LO6VIiX0AB4KI4fBrTx2Z257yjYX1nQRSIp9Eyb0YmaoIETH_yXmHrXBNDQBr5Z9vFHNW8igZ-zbaIHz_VJIU-JRL8Tk3_5kSP0iCI0xxpyBeUyfMf_Z8-THHWaqbjROnUlYcLm95jHuBDXhDyaHNJDC5P-wVZWoOtYGwL3wKbbgNqbkm4hMIyQCRRMZKhVL8SQ7cJN-45To4Ls-Xy0)  
*Figure 3.3. Intra-Device Communication Flowchart*

In the standalone configuration, the device operates as an independent host. Upon initialization, the ESP32-C3 microcontroller establishes a local Wi-Fi Access Point (AP) and launches an asynchronous web server. When a client device (smartphone or PC) connects to this AP, the system serves a static HTML/JS website stored in the external W25Q64 flash memory .

Once the session initiates, the device enters a high-priority loop: it acquires angular data from the MT6701 sensor via the Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI) , validates the signal integrity using a CRC check , and buffers the data. This data is then streamed directly to the client's browser via UDP or TCP WebSockets for real-time visualization, utilizing Client-Side Rendering (CSR) to minimize the processing load on the microcontroller.

## **3.4 Hardware Implementation**

**![][image5]**

*Figure 3.5. Schematic of the components*  
**![][image6]**  
*Figure 3.6. Visualized schematic of the components* 

The hardware architecture transforms the theoretical design into a physical prototype capable of autonomous operation. The system integration, as illustrated in the schematic diagram, prioritizes a compact footprint without compromising signal integrity or processing power. The circuit design consolidates the control unit, sensing elements, and power management subsystems into a unified modular framework suitable for wearable application.

### **3.4.1 Control Unit and Storage**

**![][image7]**

*Figure 3.7. ESP32 \- C3 SUPER MINI*

The computational core of the framework is established upon the ESP32-C3 SUPER MINI, a System-on-Chip (SoC) selected for its diminutive 26×18 mm form factor. While this compact size inherently limits the number of available General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins, the configuration is optimized to support all essential peripherals without requiring port expanders. Specific GPIO pins are strictly allocated: I2C lines drive the OLED display and current sensor, while a dedicated  Serial Peripheral Interface(SPI) bus manages high-speed communication for both the external flash memory and the magnetic encoders using a direct Chip Select (CS) topology. Beyond its processing capabilities, the ESP32-C3 was selected for its integrated Wi-Fi and ESP-NOW protocols, which are fundamental to the system's data streaming and multi-device synchronization features.

**![][image8]**

*Figure 3.8. W25QXX SPI Flash Module*

To augment the microcontroller's limited internal storage, an external W25Q64 SPI flash module is integrated, providing an additional 8 MB of non-volatile memory. This expansion is utilized exclusively to store the static assets of the web interface, such as HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files. This architecture ensures the device can independently host a rich, client-side rendered application without burdening the microcontroller’s internal program memory or requiring an active internet connection for the user interface.

Primarily, this expanded capacity provides the necessary headroom for the development of a comprehensive web interface. It allows for the embedding of detailed support resources, such as interactive user guides, directly within the device firmware, thereby reducing reliance on external documentation. Furthermore, the additional storage supports the deployment of resource-intensive JavaScript libraries required for advanced data processing and visualization. By hosting these sophisticated libraries locally, the system facilitates complex analytical operations and high-resolution graphical rendering on the client side, ensuring a robust and insightful assessment tool independent of internet connectivity.

### **3.4.2 Sensor**

**![][image9]**

*Figure 3.9. MT6701 magnetic encoder*

For precise angular acquisition, the system integrates the MT6701 magnetic encoder, a component that utilizes Hall sensing technology to determine absolute angular position based on the orientation of the magnetic field.This sensor was selected for its ability to provide absolute position data—similar to absolute gray code encoders—while maintaining the cost-effectiveness of incremental solutions. Its compact form factor of 5×6×1.7 mm allows for unobtrusive integration into the wearable housing. Furthermore, the component exhibits minimal thermal drift, rated at approximately [![][image10]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=0.12%25%2F%5E%5Ccirc%5Ctext%7BC%7D#0), which obviates the need for active thermal regulation within the device housing. While the sensor requires a specific cylindrical, diametrically magnetized magnet, compatible units are standard inclusions with locally available modules, ensuring supply chain feasibility.

Three primary communication protocols were evaluated for the sensor integration. The Analog/PWM mode was initially considered for its implementation simplicity, requiring only three lines. However, this mode was rejected due to its susceptibility to signal noise and sensor drift, alongside a limitation to 12-bit resolution, which merely matches the microcontroller's internal ADC without offering the necessary precision for kinematic analysis. Alternatively, the I2C protocol offers a higher 14-bit resolution but presents a critical architectural bottleneck: the sensor supports only two configurable addresses, and the configuration method is undocumented. To implement the required four-sensor array using I2C would necessitate the addition of external hardware multiplexers, thereby increasing circuit complexity and pin usage without adding functional value.

Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI) was selected as the optimal output mode. This protocol provides the same superior 14-bit resolution as I2C but with significantly enhanced speed and reliability. SSI supports rapid data acquisition with a communication time of approximately 2μs while the sensor has propagation delay of only 5μs, ensuring that the system captures high-velocity joint movements without phase lag. Critically, the SSI implementation in the MT6701 includes a Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) and magnetic field status bits within the data frame. These features allow the firmware to actively verify signal integrity and detect magnetic misalignment, resulting in a robust data stream that is resistant to the electromagnetic interference often present in clinical environments.

### **3.4.3 Power Management and Storage**

**![][image11]**

*Figure 3.10. TP4057 Charging Module*

The power management subsystem is designed to ensure operational safety, efficient energy conversion, and precise run-time estimation for the wearable device. Battery charging is managed by the TP4057, a linear charger specifically engineered for single-cell lithium-ion chemistries. This module ensures the longevity of the power source by strictly regulating charging current and voltage, effectively safeguarding the system against thermal and electrical stress.

![][image12]

*Figure 3.11. LiPo 402050 3.7 400mAh*

The device is powered by a 402050 Lithium Polymer (LiPo) battery with a rated capacity of 400 mAh, selected for its lightweight form factor which minimizes the mechanical load on the wearable harness. To further ensure operational safety, the battery includes an integrated protection circuit that actively prevents common failure modes such as overcharging, over-discharging, and short circuits.

![][image13]

*Figure 3.12. XL63070 buck-boost converter*

Voltage regulation is achieved through an XL63070 buck-boost converter, which maintains a stable 3.3 V logic level for the microcontroller and sensors regardless of the battery’s fluctuating voltage profile. This switching regulator was chosen over linear alternatives for its superior efficiency, which maximizes the device's operational duration on a single charge.

![][image14]

*Figure 3.13. INA219 Current & Power Sensor*

To provide the user with accurate battery status, the system incorporates an INA219 high-side current and power sensor. Unlike simple voltage dividers, this sensor measures real-time current flow, enabling the firmware to implement a Coulomb Counting algorithm. This approach, calibrated against the manufacturer's discharge curve, provides a precise estimation of the State-of-Charge (SOC), ensuring reliable data regarding the device's remaining operating time.

## 

## **3.5 Communication Protocols and Firmware**

### **3.5.1 Synchronous Serial System** 

Communication between the microcontroller and the MT6701 sensors utilizes the Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI). SSI was chosen over [![][image15]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=I%5E2C#0) to avoid addressing conflicts and the need for multiplexers. It provides high-speed data transfer ([![][image16]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=~2%5Cmu%20s#0) communication time) with Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) for enhanced data integrity.

### **3.5.2 TCP/UDP (Wireless Streaming)**

The buffered data must then be packaged for transmission over Wi-Fi. The management of this data depends entirely on the chosen fidelity mode, using either the UDP or TCP protocol.

The Low Fidelity, Low Latency (LFLL) Mode is designed to prioritize real-time visualization using the User Datagram Protocol (UDP). The method leverages UDP's connectionless nature, sending data packets immediately without confirmation to minimize delay. The safe payload limit for a UDP packet is approximately 1472 bytes, which allows for about 184 readings from a single sensor or approximately 11 complete time-steps from a maximum 16-sensor setup. This speed, however, involves a trade-off in reliability; packet loss is expected. Consequently, this "low fidelity" mode is ideal for live monitoring but unsuitable for reliable data recording.

The High Fidelity, High Latency (HFHL) Mode is designed to prioritize data integrity for analysis by utilizing the connection-oriented TCP protocol via WebSockets. This method ensures "high fidelity" because TCP guarantees that every packet is delivered in the correct order and without errors. Instead of streaming immediately, the device buffers readings in its DRAM for a 1-3 second timeframe, aggregating this data into a larger block which is then sent in bulk over the reliable TCP connection. The trade-off for this reliable, error-checking process is a noticeable delay, or latency, making it unsuitable for real-time feedback but essential for capturing a complete and accurate dataset required for clinical records and post-session analysis.

### **3.5.3 Telemetry Optimization via Bit-Packing**

To ensure data completeness without compromising the strict latency requirements of real-time biofeedback, the firmware employs a bit-packing strategy that consolidates all critical telemetry into a standardized 64-bit (8-byte) primitive. This compact binary format provides a significant advantage over verbose text-based protocols (such as JSON), maximizing wireless throughput to the client application while minimizing the processing overhead on the microcontroller.

The payload is meticulously structured to maximize information density for the single Central Node architecture. The first segment occupies 14 bits, storing the absolute angular position directly from the wired sensor array. This raw resolution is essential for capturing the minute motor fluctuations associated with spasticity or tremors, data often lost in lower-resolution systems.

To guarantee precise temporal alignment between the physical movement and the Digital Avatar displayed on the web interface, a full 32-bit timestamp is appended. Unlike traditional systems that rely on the receiving device to timestamp arrival, this device-side timestamping allows the web application to reconstruct the exact sequence of motion with microsecond precision, ensuring that the visual feedback seen by the patient is perfectly synchronized with their actual exertion.

The remaining capacity is utilized to enhance system diagnostics and clinical safety. The State-of-Charge (SoC) field utilizes 8 bits, enabling the system to report battery levels with 0.4% resolution—critical for ensuring the device does not power down unexpectedly during a patient assessment. Finally, the remaining 10 bits are reserved for status flags, such as magnetic error warnings (indicating the sensor has slipped) or charging states, ensuring the protocol remains extensible for future clinical features without requiring a complete redesign.

## 

## **3.6 Software Architecture**

The firmware is developed in C++ utilizing a component-based Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) approach to ensure modularity and scalability. This structure utilizes a Finite State Machine (FSM) to strictly manage the device's operational modes, data acquisition pipeline, and user interface, ensuring a deterministic response to clinical inputs. 

### **3.6.1 Libraries**

	The system architecture leverages a specific suite of optimized libraries to manage hardware peripherals and data streaming. To ensure high-speed communication with the magnetic sensors, the firmware integrates MT6701\_SSI, a custom driver developed specifically for this study to manage the Synchronous Serial Interface protocol. Network connectivity is managed by the standard ESP-IDF WiFi stack (via WiFi.h), which handles the Soft-AP generation and TCP/UDP socket connections required for real-time data streaming. The web-based clinical interface is instantiated via the ESPAsyncWebServer library to allow for non-blocking, asynchronous HTTP request handling, while the U8g2 graphics library is employed to drive the OLED display for visualizing battery status and IP configuration without interrupting the main processing loop.

### 

### **3.6.2 Classes & Objects**

| class Component { }; // \--- Protocols \--- class I2C : public Component { }; class SSI : public Component { }; // \--- Specific Peripherals \--- class Encoder : public SSI { }; class PowerSensor : public I2C { }; class Display : public I2C { }; class ExternalROM : public SSI { }; |
| :---- |

*Figure 3.14. Component Classes*

The software architecture leverages Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) to enforce hardware abstraction and modularity. As shown in the class structure above, the Component class serves as the abstract base for all hardware peripherals. Protocols such as I2C and SSI inherit directly from this base, encapsulating the specific driver logic required for communication (e.g., clock timing and pin management). Specific peripherals, such as the Encoder and ExternalROM, allow the main application to interact with hardware without needing to manage low-level bit-banging manually.

| class Device {   public:     // \--- Hardware Objects (Composition) \---     Encoder encoders\[4\];     PowerSensor powerSensor;     Display display;     ExternalROM externalRom;     // \--- UI Page Objects (Composition) \---     MainPage mainPage;     PairingPage pairingPage;     ReadingsPage readingsPage;     SettingsPage settingsPage; };  |
| :---- |

*Figure 3.15. Device Classes*

The Device class acts as the primary container, utilizing composition to own instances of the hardware objects (Encoder, Display) and UI pages (MainPage, ReadingsPage). This ensures that a device object is self-contained with all its necessary drivers initialized upon startup. 

### 

### **3.6.3 State Machine**

[![][image17]](https://www.plantuml.com/plantuml/img/RPBFQy8m5CVl-IiUNheTkkbjo1XMALDGaVQmW-oGDMzjC4rA-gguiVzzQJMrWntVzyVdXKTcDN5BJQbOVP2wufQNeAH6EbS8nb9XM2450fOLdK5WdLbPaJIQiG0c3x3QBf8uIcD8Tz5YjNs5PPp4pjB6PR62p6YoHjLG62k_tC0L2EcMwWpX0-c4g5r8DQMk1q_mzSbux62MwyCVs14joUm_b3Md8SStdyPieBeE8NmpW3dF3harZHOBeuo5jq8Ijhgn0kr5coiNSbfa7Nme_JXcHngbI2HrNiEEvpW2NeDNV6B3fOODwWRkLaBXVHTgPn-8Cf97X0IvQ7kTxmVM7djvE-2Qdqr3qtu9F8VxDNxIUtXP4UuJcHTkVBaXBePogT39VRE5ACm80Yy5h3T3ssBQVDUUTkTYLrJl-HL3Llkf0jOx_Tuh9oLJmUCLnsQeXVk1lm00)	

*Figure 3.16. System Control Finite State Machine* 

The high-level logic is governed by a Finite State Machine (FSM) that manages user interaction and system modes. The device defaults to an Idle (Main Menu) state, where power consumption is minimized. User input triggers transitions to specific sub-states: Active Readings locks the interface to dedicate resources to the data pipeline. The system returns to Idle only upon a manual "Stop" command or a "Back" navigation event, ensuring that critical processes like data streaming are not interrupted accidentally.

[![][image18]](https://www.plantuml.com/plantuml/img/TLJVJzim47xFNt7YOS1414NJDbMeefHrgaH7rNO32NXmualZrR63xQpBfkrltzbEAGBjfMhkpjztzzsFS-kOSKqb4xiMgcQ6LI25GjVM2DguKYUbu0XOrQu5ZZOtedP2gsVn1E4GVYS05onVhunk51zfgGtSbCAXjsl3qKJRXQGWiWsDqPiN9kUCo1g7OwtSGln2w9sGUVTrcln9aaYtjt1CyQpT0sPXmYL27ywo0LbTO_jdnza0RfXmGgsW88e5MajPtokAcUaDcXXofJTmi41bjR5mNHI7J_33t8aV29VCCPY96hqeWMlx4Ge623_Rb1OJc2FZS31TllzmqZiC3u8b9bSZyaZyz_IaiS3onqPOuRQPpT5gsVZF6DLxbsR2mJ0BPdnDA5QAIPWeXoiZg46Z4lDrO1tDHv5qA5UQV6KL8Rtz3KpPQYnGSeXwlGJEUt1sbfTQv3WO9CyPvrxGYwOeq0IQ-3SoJR7Ifeqq7q_M6LpEXrCOJyRNeJYlwBQmJ-gnmGPXHer7zxekxSsml0qSozk8FpFQwLpB2F7rSdQy7Cq8efMQyLSKOzdO4fo6aHIeNAnYFqrt3HnBwdwQ1aTeKfeEEjtcR1DQ7tpUv5rvRvVeFetEIkHzc1J1Np0XxH5mONDcED1eWa5dMX1LXLnGLR8D3qdQjAa9hHiB_tXCZ_yFNJDhaR2pABKh4VWsDOz6M4zTkFl6fE0XzOSTOkVsaK7BJhAGGlkSU4dOLrhNVVYi8QFUUFr2edw7FBF2dmuAsleuZ0INxchvufttQYagJE_Lmc7jzzLhlcYjmmgMXgauxjOBV_Vs8JptwqjnWTuzXO2W1G5ROouAaKD9cjgIhV48b7RmlR4EzdAj5EP-o_UIS0IUPTEjFFXCwCPv0bBK5s7ZU6K6sPg4tQ3lLMEKhzR6iv9qbNMNPTJn4Dn8QekSKEayWYzLDuHzZehJ5Vq7)

*Figure 3.17. Active Data Pipeline*

When in the "Active Readings" state, the system executes the pipeline illustrated above. The process begins with an SSI Read from the MT6701 sensor, capturing a 14-bit absolute angle. This raw data passes through a Signal Integrity Check, which validates the CRC and magnetic field status; invalid frames are immediately discarded to prevent data corruption. Validated data is then pushed into a Ring Buffer (80kb DRAM FIFO) to decouple the high-speed sensor acquisition from the slower Wi-Fi transmission. Finally, the Socket TX task retrieves this buffered data and flushes it to the client via UDP or TCP before the system returns to the reading loop.

### **3.6.4 System Operational Sequence**

To minimize the computational burden on the microcontroller, the user interface is implemented as a static web application stored within the external W25Q64 flash memory. The system adopts a Client-Side Rendering (CSR) architecture, where the ESP32-C3 functions primarily as a file server for the initial handshake and a data pump during active sessions. By offloading the resource-intensive tasks of graphical rendering and real-time data visualization to the client device—typically a smartphone or personal computer—the firmware ensures that the microcontroller's limited processing cycles are dedicated exclusively to high-speed sensor acquisition and wireless transmission.

[![][image19]](https://www.plantuml.com/plantuml/img/ZLHjQzim4FxkNt4mQ9r0shAFAMnruwJDNbeJEuJ1O2ZM9JuYIvcarEl8Zz_PJaPercOI-8FkdfTxPFl6UM7zfbI1Mv5U2ojAk1NvQcdDHik1KSR2h22F1_Kez_H4_lc_-uoLQDkUMyMWmnvhJVLAQGUFIPH6osEMu1nYN916I2igbT3eWksxaQZtTg08jUU69yfno-V3KRstNoct9ZVQMwCKMWZlZVCG9Psw9sxMDat6C5B25P1wOyMIyS5BmYQte3ymMcFkmHkOKMz4p7JDpM-ez2FdQAbXd40afKNd88pl1qdd-bYaFy6V6sJa3EUEmsMUB4lWRfZ1PUUKpXvBMkAlYyAN2ix0-MU55xbpDVeVSp9AI9Y82aQa46BXnIcT5EqJNu4NdlAzqUywTAWbL3lV2oPpiB2cX4Kj-CgmxDJ2T7pvAOMmhVPIaiZThC9q0rEMGii-TBjf5aso7vDX58yVxhhTKoR7cZm9HR_PAMfdhECmCYhHkZrvbA-qgHJA9UVy_keA7Z_NDiyXAzeWu8inwy3Y6eKFW5SRLiktYwevRkWQH3YD4-1YrjnlNMmUBqVVaUL8dEGPJDTI-5hJ5NCZh3n4lZ5awmogehwvCCqUatrWiEEM7pj0_Buzc4uGz3x0DwodZyc9kVeRp8ayZZy43YMuOGJ_HVu0)

*Figure 3.19. Wireless Data Streaming*

Interaction between the hosted interface and the device is governed strictly through an Application Programming Interface (API). The web application communicates with the firmware using standard HTTP requests for command and control operations, such as initiating sensor calibration or triggering the start of a recording session. For the transmission of kinematic data, the system establishes a persistent connection to stream raw sensor values. This approach allows the browser to interpret the binary stream and dynamically generate the visual output, such as live graphs or skeletal tracking, without requiring server-side processing. Consequently, the software design prioritizes functional data management and wireless device control, serving as a functional tool rather than a graphically intensive application.

### **3.6.5 Algorithms**

The reliability of the system relies on a set of core algorithms designed to manage signal integrity, power estimation, and data buffering. These algorithmic processes are implemented directly within the firmware to ensure that the hardware constraints—specifically the high-speed sensor interface and the asynchronous nature of wireless transmission—do not compromise the accuracy of the kinematic data.

#### **3.6.5.1 Signal Integrity Verification via Cyclic Redundancy Check**

The integrity of the angular data retrieved from the MT6701 sensors is critical, particularly given the high-speed nature of the Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI) utilized in this architecture. To mitigate the risk of data corruption caused by electromagnetic interference or signal attenuation, the firmware implements a signal verification process based on a 24-bit data frame structure. Upon receiving a frame, the system parses the signal into three distinct segments: the first 14 bits represent the absolute angular position, the subsequent 4 bits indicate the magnetic field status, and the final 6 bits contain the Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) code.

To validate this data, the system algorithmically computes a theoretical checksum from the first 18 bits of the payload using the generator polynomial: 

[![][image20]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%20P\(x\)%3Dx6%2Bx%2B1%20#0) 

This computed value is then compared against the received 6-bit CRC code. If a discrepancy is detected, the entire frame is classified as a transmission error and is immediately discarded to prevent noise artifacts from contaminating the kinematic dataset. Concurrently, the algorithm evaluates the 4-bit magnetic field status to verify that the magnet remains within the optimal Z-axis range; if the status indicates a weak or missing magnetic field, the system flags a hardware fault, ensuring that only physically valid readings are processed.

**3.6.5.2 State-of-Charge Estimation via Coulomb Counting**

Accurate power monitoring is essential for portable medical instrumentation, yet traditional voltage-based estimation proves insufficient for the Lithium Polymer (LiPo) batteries used in this device. LiPo chemistries exhibit non-linear discharge curves and are prone to significant voltage sag under the high current loads required by Wi-Fi transmission. To address this, the system employs a Coulomb Counting algorithm utilizing the INA219 current sensor. Rather than relying on instantaneous voltage, the firmware estimates the State-of-Charge (SOC) by mathematically integrating the net current flux over time. This process is defined by the equation:

[![][image21]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%20SOC\(t\)%20%3D%20SOC\(t_0\)%20-%20%5Cfrac%7B1%7D%7BC_%7Brated%7D%7D%20%5Cint_%7Bt_0%7D%5E%7Bt%7D%20I\(t\)%20%5C%2C%20dt%20#0) , 

Where Crated​ represents the nominal battery capacity of 400 mAh. By continuously tracking the energy consumed from a known initial state, this integration method provides a stable and accurate estimation of remaining runtime. This approach effectively filters out the temporary voltage fluctuations caused by wireless data bursts, providing the user with a consistent power metric that reflects the true energy capacity remaining in the cell.

#### **3.6.5.3 Asynchronous Data Buffering (Ring Buffer)**

A significant bandwidth asymmetry exists between the sensor acquisition rate and the Wi-Fi transmission interval. The sensors are capable of acquiring data at approximately 20μs per read, a rate significantly faster than the network stack can transmit packets. To bridge this temporal gap and prevent data loss during network congestion, the firmware utilizes a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) Ring Buffer algorithm. An 80kb segment of the microcontroller's DRAM is allocated for this purpose, serving as a circular queue capable of storing approximately 2,500 kinematic frames.

The algorithm operates on a producer-consumer model where the high-priority interrupt service routine acts as the producer, pushing validated 24-bit SSI frames to the head of the buffer at the native sensor rate. Asynchronously, the network task acts as the consumer, polling the buffer to retrieve aggregated data chunks for transmission via TCP or UDP. In the event of buffer saturation, the algorithm implements a "drop-oldest" policy, overwriting the tail of the buffer. This ensures that even in high-latency network conditions, the data stream prioritizes the most current physiological state of the patient, maintaining the relevance of the real-time visualization.

#### **3.6.5.4 Zero-Position Calibration and Offset Correction**

While the MT6701 provides an absolute angular reading ([![][image22]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%5Ctheta_%7Braw%7D#0)) ranging from [![][image23]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=0#0) to [![][image24]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=2%5E%7B14%7D-1#0) (0 to 16383), this electrical angle rarely aligns perfectly with the mechanical neutral position (0°) of the human joint due to mounting variances. To establish a clinically relevant frame of reference, the system employs a Zero-Position Calibration algorithm.
