Upon a user-initiated calibration command (typically performed when the subject is in a neutral anatomical pose), the system captures the current raw sensor value as the offset constant ([![][image25]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Ctheta_%7Boffset%7D#0)). All subsequent readings are adjusted relative to this zero point. The corrected angle ([![][image26]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Ctheta_%7Bcorr%7D#0)) is calculated using modulo arithmetic to handle the "wrap-around" point where the sensor transitions from 360° back to 0°:

[![][image27]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Ctheta_%7Bcorr%7D%20%3D%20\(%5Ctheta_%7Braw%7D%20-%20%5Ctheta_%7Boffset%7D\)%20%5Cpmod%7BM%7D#0)

Where [![][image28]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=M#0) is the sensor resolution ([![][image29]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=2%5E%7B14%7D%20%3D%2016384#0)). However, standard modulo operations can result in negative values when [![][image30]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Ctheta_%7Braw%7D%20%3C%20%5Ctheta_%7Boffset%7D#0). To ensure a strictly positive index that can be correctly mapped to physical degrees, the formula is expanded:

[![][image31]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Ctheta_%7Bcorr%7D%20%3D%20%5Cbigg\(%20\(%5Ctheta_%7Braw%7D%20-%20%5Ctheta_%7Boffset%7D\)%20%2B%20M%20%5Cbigg\)%20%5Cpmod%7BM%7D#0)

Finally, this corrected integer value is converted into physical degrees ([![][image32]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Cphi_%7Bdeg%7D#0)):

[![][image33]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Cphi_%7Bdeg%7D%20%3D%20%5Ctheta_%7Bcorr%7D%20%5Ctimes%20%5Cleft\(%20%5Cfrac%7B360%5E%7B%5Ccirc%7D%7D%7B16384%7D%20%5Cright\)#0)

This algorithm ensures that regardless of the initial magnet orientation, the software outputs a clean [![][image34]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=0.00%5E%5Ccirc#0) reading when the limb is in the neutral position, simplifying clinical interpretation.

#### **3.6.5.5 Asynchronous Data Buffering (Ring Buffer)**

A significant bandwidth asymmetry exists between the sensor acquisition rate and the Wi-Fi transmission interval. The sensors are capable of acquiring data at approximately [![][image35]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=20%5C%2C%5Cmu%5Ctext%7Bs%7D#0) per read ([![][image36]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bacq%7D#0)), a rate significantly faster than the network stack can transmit packets ([![][image37]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=t_%7Btx%7D#0)). To bridge this temporal gap and prevent data loss during network congestion, the firmware utilizes a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) Ring Buffer algorithm.

An 80kb segment of the microcontroller's DRAM is allocated for this purpose, serving as a circular queue capable of storing approximately 2,500 kinematic frames ([![][image38]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=N_%7Bcapacity%7D#0)). The algorithm operates on a producer-consumer model where the high-priority interrupt service routine acts as the producer, and the network task acts as the consumer. The data flow is managed by two index pointers, [![][image39]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=P_%7Bhead%7D#0) (write) and [![][image40]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=P_%7Btail%7D#0) (read). When a new 24-bit SSI frame is acquired, the head pointer position is calculated using modulo arithmetic to ensure the circular wrapping property:

[![][image41]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=P_%7Bhead\(next\)%7D%20%3D%20\(P_%7Bhead%7D%20%2B%201\)%20%5Cpmod%7BN_%7Bcapacity%7D%7D#0)

Before writing, the algorithm checks for buffer saturation to prevent memory corruption. The buffer is defined as "Full" if the next head position coincides with the current tail position:

[![][image42]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%20%5Ctext%7BBuffer%20Full%7D%20%5CLongleftrightarrow%20P_%7Bhead\(next\)%7D%20%3D%3D%20P_%7Btail%7D%20#0)

In the event of buffer saturation (network congestion), the algorithm implements a "drop-oldest" policy. The system forcibly increments the tail pointer to overwrite the oldest data, ensuring that the live stream always reflects the most current physiological state of the patient. The update logic is defined as:

[![][image43]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=P_%7Btail%7D%20%3D%20%5Cbegin%7Bcases%7D%20\(P_%7Btail%7D%20%2B%201\)%20%5Cpmod%7BN_%7Bcapacity%7D%7D%20%26%20%5Ctext%7Bif%20Buffer%20Full%7D%20%5C%5C%5C%5C%20P_%7Btail%7D%20%26%20%5Ctext%7Botherwise%7D%20%5Cend%7Bcases%7D#0)

This mechanism effectively decouples the hard real-time requirements of the sensor network from the variable latency of the wireless interface.

#### **3.6.6 Clinical Web Interface and Features** **![][image44]**

 To address the clinical constraint of strict time efficiency, the user interface (UI) is designed as a progressive web application (PWA) that consolidates the entire assessment workflow—from setup to documentation—into a single screen. This interface implements specific features derived from PT interviews to enhance diagnostic utility and reduce administrative burden.

#### **3.6.6.1 Real-Time Visualization and Biofeedback**

![][image45]  
![][image46]  
 The application utilizes the Three.js library to render a digital twin of the patient’s limb in real-time. Unlike static numerical displays, this 3D avatar provides immediate visual biofeedback, allowing both the clinician and patient to visualize movement quality during the assessment. Asynchronously, a live charting library renders the angular waveform (degrees vs. time), enabling the clinician to identify smoothness of motion and potential tremors (spasticity) as they occur.

#### 

#### **3.6.6.2 Automated Normative Feedback**

![][image47]  
To support clinical decision-making, the software integrates an automated comparison logic against AAOS (American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons) normative data. During an assessment, if a patient’s Range of Motion (ROM) falls significantly below the age-adjusted norm (e.g., \<120° knee flexion), the interface provides a visual alert (color-coded highlighting). This immediate feedback alerts the clinician to potential pathology without requiring manual reference checks during the session.

#### **3.6.6.3 Session Persistence and Replayability**

 A key limitation of manual goniometry is the inability to review a movement once it has occurred. To address this, the software implements a "Session DVR" feature. All kinematic data is cached in the browser’s IndexedDB. Upon completion of a test, clinicians can scrub through the session timeline, pause at specific frames, and analyze the gait cycle frame-by-frame. This allows for a detailed post-hoc analysis of transient events, such as a "catch" in motion, which might be missed during real-time observation.

#### **3.6.6.4 Post-Processing Offset Correction** 

Recognizing that physical sensor placement may shift during dynamic tasks (Soft Tissue Artifact), the software includes a non-destructive post-processing tool. If a clinician detects that the sensor harness shifted during the test, they can apply a "Virtual Offset" to the recorded data. This software-defined zeroing realigns the dataset to the anatomical neutral position without requiring the patient to undergo the physical assessment again, thereby preserving clinical efficiency.

#### **3.6.6.5 Automated Document Generation**

To meet the performance metric of report generation in under one minute, the system integrates a client-side PDF generator (jspdf). With a single click, the software compiles the patient’s demographic data, the calculated Max/Min ROM, and the waveform graphs into a standardized clinical report. This automates the previously manual transcription process, ensuring that documentation is completed instantly at the point of care.

## **3.7 Mechanical Design and Kinematics**

### **3.7.1 Anatomical Analysis**

[![][image48]](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Human_skeleton_front_en.svg/330px-Human_skeleton_front_en.svg.png?20190526032357)  
*Figure 3.20. Human skeleton front*  
*(https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Human\_skeleton\_front\_en.svg)*

The design focuses on the Gross Motor Joints—specifically the elbow-wrist-shoulder and knee-hip-ankle complexes. These joints were selected because they produce the largest range of motion and share similar mechanical pivot mechanisms. The Elbow and Knee are centered in the target configurations; therefore, the central sensor node is positioned at these landmarks. Shoulder and Hips have multiple axes of motion but we will focus on the abduction/adduction and flexion/extension. Our selected sensor technology cannot effectively measure rotational motions, and will increase complexity of mounting and linkage for the exoskeletal framework. However, they are best measured with magnetometers, which is out of the scope for the study but we suggest future iteration of this study to implement this. For wrist joints, we will only focus on flexion and extension. For ankle joints we will also only focus dorsiflexion and plantar flexion. The radial and ulnar deviation of wrist as well as inversion and eversion of ankle is also better sensed with IMU technology. These are the current limitations of this study for setting the scope to only use 1 sensor technology. We recommend future iterations of this work to include magnetometers in sensor nodes. 

### **3.7.2 Kinematic Alignment via Exoskeletal Emulation**

Measurement of the joints is a challenge because skin does not move with the bones directly. Attaching to skin and full body suits like those used in motion capture systems still report losing accuracy due to skin movements. The inaccuracies are fine in those settings but fall short in physical therapy. Inaccuracy and inconsistency issues are always present in ROM measurements because it's hard to align the measurement tools. So our assumption for the design and development of our goniometer centers around augmenting the skeleton on the outside, in other words an exoskeleton. 

Augmenting the skeletal features of the human body increases the accuracy and placement of measuring devices because it follows the principle of a joint being constrained to another joint. The foundational studies to be used for this are also well established, specifically kinematics and multibody dynamics.   
	Exoskeleton works well for ROM measurements but historical development of Motion Capture systems stayed away from this type of configuration because of its clunkiness, stiffness, heaviness, and difficulty in moving freely. But we can optimize this by making our goniometer as small as possible and as lightweight as possible. Connecting rods for the goniometer to be constrained to another joint limits the freedom of movements so we are to design a connecting element that can bend but also be stiff when needed. 

![][image49]

*Figure 3.21. Flexure of a ruler*  
*(https://www.open.edu/openlearn/science-maths-technology/design-innovation/design/content-section-6.5)*

Such a type of movement does not really need advanced technology, in fact many of us use it, rulers. Flexible rulers bend in many ways but will always be stiff when applied force in a specific way. Much like our goniometer that pivots uniaxially, rulers are most stiff in only one axis. This is very useful and will solve problems the exoskeleton type motion capture systems face which is constraining the freedom of movement. 

### **3.7.3 Proposed Device and Sensor Node Design**

**![][image50]**

*Figure 3.22. Central Node of the Device*

The Central Sensor Node functions as the primary control and power unit of the Device, designed to align with the intermediate joints of the extremities (elbow and knee). Mechanically, it utilizes a uniaxial pivot design featuring a dual-arm sandwich construction with a central bearing to ensure smooth articulation and consistent magnetic alignment during flexion and extension. Electronically, this node houses the complete active hardware stack: the ESP32-C3 microcontroller, the primary MT6701 magnetic encoder, the LiPo battery, and the power management circuitry. It features physical interface ports designed to receive wired connections from External Sensor Nodes, allowing the Central Node to aggregate synchronized multi-joint kinematic data before transmitting the payload wirelessly to the network master.

The External Sensor Nodes are engineered as passive peripheral units designed for the proximal and distal joints (Shoulder/Hip and Wrist/Ankle). To minimize inertial mass on the extremities, these nodes do not contain internal power sources or microcontrollers; they house only the magnetic encoder and the requisite mechanical gearing.

**![][image51]**

*Figure 3.23. Pitch Type External Sensor Node* 

The "Pitch-Type" external node is designed to capture motions orthogonal to the main device's axis, such as shoulder abduction. To eliminate the mechanical backlash and friction inherent in bevel gear systems, this iteration utilizes a Direct-Chip-on-Pivot architecture. The MT6701 Hall-effect sensor is decoupled from its carrier PCB and mounted directly onto the mechanical pivot axis, ensuring perfect concentric alignment with the magnet. The sensor is electrically tethered to the local signal board using ultra-fine, flexible enameled copper wire. This configuration allows the sensing element to track complex, multi-planar rotations with zero mechanical hysteresis while maintaining a compact, low-profile housing.

**![][image52]**

*Figure 3.24. Yaw Type External Sensor Node*

For flexion and extension measurements at the proximal or distal joints, the system utilizes a "Yaw-Type" External Sensor Node. Unlike previous iterations, this variant employs a direct-coupling mechanism where the sensor chip is positioned immediately over the joint’s center of rotation. By desoldering the sensor from the rigid PCB and reconnecting it via flexible enamel wiring, the design isolates the sensing element from mechanical stress and allows it to float precisely within the magnetic field of the diametrically magnetized magnet. This direct integration removes the need for spur gears, significantly reducing the node's weight and eliminating the accumulation of mechanical error during rapid directional changes. Like the pitch node, this unit transmits high-resolution angular data back to the Central Node through the wired Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI).

## **3.8 Sensor Node Placement and Alignment**

The proposed system is a versatile, modular electrogoniometer designed for assessing any gross motor joint. To ensure clinical reliability, the hardware employs a body-attached placement protocol. While the central node functions as a standalone universal goniometer, the system's capacity for synchronized multi-joint data acquisition is evaluated in this study using a specific configuration for lower-extremity gait analysis. This configuration serves to validate the modular architecture by capturing temporally aligned movement patterns across the hip, knee, and ankle joints  
**3.8.1 Universal Central Node Placement**

The Central Node is the goniometer device in of itself. For maximum versatility, it is designed to be used for all gross motor joints. This universal compatibility allows the clinician to deploy the device for various clinical needs, ranging from upper-extremity movements to complex lower-body assessments.

### **3.8.2 Multi-Joint Configuration for Gait Analysis**

When expanded for the multi-joint requirements of gait analysis, the system utilizes modular external nodes of both pitch and yaw types to capture coordinated movement. At the hip joint, two distinct measurements are captured through a dual-node setup: a pitch-type node is placed lateral to the greater trochanter to measure abduction and adduction in the frontal plane, while a yaw-type node tracks flexion and extension in the sagittal plane. To complete the lower-extremity profile, a single yaw-type node is aligned with the lateral malleolus to monitor ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. This specialized 4-measurement arrangement allows the system to synthesize a holistic view of gait mechanics, tracking how proximal hip stability and distal ankle control interact during functional ambulation.

![][image53]

*Figure 3.26. Central sensor node placed at the knee joint*

![][image54]

*Figure 3.28 Pitch sensor placed at hip joint*

![][image55]

*Figure 3.29. Pitch Sensor node paced ankle joint*

![][image56]

*Figure 3.32. Yaw sensor placed at hip joint*

#### **3.8.2 Exoskeletal Framework and Flexible Linkages**

To address the inherent issue of "Bio-Joint Mismatch"—where the instant center of rotation of a human joint shifts during movement—the system utilizes an Exoskeletal Emulation approach. Rather than relying solely on skin adhesion, the sensor nodes are connected via flexible static linkages. These linkages, constructed from semi-rigid material similar to a thin ruler, are stiff in the measurement axis to transmit rotational force accurately but compliant in orthogonal axes. This design creates a "floating" exoskeletal frame that parallels the user's skeletal structure. The linkages maintain a fixed distance between the central and external nodes, effectively augmenting the human skeleton with a measurable, external kinematic chain.

### **3.8.3 Geometric and Mechanical Design Constraints** 

While the linkages provide static alignment, the mounting interface must accommodate dynamic muscle expansion and skin deformation. Rigid strapping would constrict the limb and introduce measurement artifacts. Therefore, the mounting mechanism is engineered to couple the static linkages to the dynamic surface of the skin while addressing three critical geometric phenomena.

#### **3.8.3.1 Surface Arc Discrepancy (The "Fillet Effect")** 

[![][image57]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1729/12/2/188)  
*Figure 3.33. The FE movement from a sagittal view \[83\]*

A fundamental geometric constraint arises in "Pitch-Type" configurations (Wrist/Shoulder). As the joint flexes, the dorsal skin surface expands, effectively increasing the localized radius of the joint and creating a "fillet" profile rather than a sharp hinge. A static, flush-mounted interface would inherently conflict with this changing arc length. The interface must therefore allow for "micro-sliding" to compensate for this variance without misaligning the sensor axis.

#### **3.8.3.2 Conical Limb Geometry (Variable Diameter)** 

![][image58]  
*Figure 3.34. Conical Limb Geometry (Variable Diameter)*

The central mounting locations (Thigh/Upper Arm) function geometrically as truncated cones rather than cylinders. Placing a linear device against a tapered limb introduces an inherent pitch deviation, causing the sensor's rotational axis to tilt away from the sagittal plane. To ensure accurate kinematic data, the interface requires a compensation mechanism capable of neutralizing this taper, ensuring the encoder remains orthogonal to the axis of motion regardless of the subject's anthropometry.

#### **3.8.3.3 Dynamic Inertial Slippage** 

![][image59]  
*Figure 3.35. Dynamic Inertial Slippage*

During rapid limb acceleration (ballistic movement), the cantilevered External Sensor Nodes are susceptible to inertial swing. Because the skin and underlying muscle move independently of the bone (Soft Tissue Artifact), the sensor node risks oscillating relative to the skeletal landmark. The mounting system must therefore prioritize coupling stability at these distal pivots to prevent "macro-slippage" and minimize the pendulum effect caused by soft tissue compliance.

## 

## **3.9 Theoretical Performance Analysis**

### **3.9.1 Power Analysis**

To evaluate the feasibility of the 402050 Lithium Polymer (LiPo) battery under realistic clinical conditions, a "Intermittent Usage" profile is established as the baseline. This profile simulates a typical one-hour rehabilitation session consisting of 30 distinct reading instances, with each instance requiring 30 seconds of continuous data acquisition. This results in a duty cycle characterized by 15 minutes (900 seconds) of active high-load operation and 45 minutes (2700 seconds) of idle operation, during which the device remains in a "Standby" state (waiting for user input via the main menu).

The power consumption is modeled by calculating the weighted average current draw ([![][image60]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=I_%7Bavg%7D#0)) based on these two distinct states. During the "Active" state ([![][image61]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bactive%7D#0)), the system powers the ESP32-C3 microcontroller, four MT6701 sensors, and the OLED display, resulting in a peak load ([![][image62]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=I_%7Bactive%7D#0)) of approximately 164 mA. Conversely, during the "Idle" state ([![][image63]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bidle%7D#0)), the firmware deactivates the sensors and shifts the microcontroller into a lower power mode while maintaining the display, reducing the load ([![][image64]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=I_%7Bidle%7D#0)) to an estimated 35 mA. The average current consumption over the one-hour session is calculated using the following equation:

[![][image65]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=I_%7Bavg%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B\(I_%7Bactive%7D%20%5Ccdot%20t_%7Bactive%7D\)%20%2B%20\(I_%7Bidle%7D%20%5Ccdot%20t_%7Bidle%7D\)%7D%7Bt_%7Btotal%7D%7D#0)

Substituting the baseline values:

[![][image66]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=I_%7Bavg%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B\(164%20%5Ctext%7B%20mA%7D%20%5Ccdot%20900%20%5Ctext%7B%20s%7D\)%20%2B%20\(35%20%5Ctext%7B%20mA%7D%20%5Ccdot%202700%20%5Ctext%7B%20s%7D\)%7D%7B3600%20%5Ctext%7B%20s%7D%7D%20%5Capprox%2067.25%20%5Ctext%7B%20mA%7D#0)

The theoretical battery life ([![][image67]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=T_%7Blife%7D#0)) is then derived by applying the DC-DC converter's efficiency factor ([![][image68]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%5Ceta%20%3D%200.90#0)) to the battery's rated capacity ([![][image69]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=C_%7Brated%7D%20%3D%20400%20%5Ctext%7B%20mAh%7D#0)) and dividing by the calculated average load:

[![][image70]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=T_%7Blife%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7BC_%7Brated%7D%20%5Ccdot%20%5Ceta%7D%7BI_%7Bavg%7D%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B400%20%5Ctext%7B%20mAh%7D%20%5Ccdot%200.90%7D%7B67.25%20%5Ctext%7B%20mA%7D%7D%20%5Capprox%205.35%20%5Ctext%7B%20hours%7D#0)

This analysis indicates that the device can sustain approximately 5.35 hours of operation under the defined clinical usage profile. This duration equates to over five complete rehabilitation sessions on a single charge. Consequently, the power system design demonstrates sufficient capacity to handle a full day of clinical throughput without requiring mid-day recharging, validating the selection of the compact 402050 battery for this application.

### **3.9.2 Data Transfer Analysis**

The data throughput capability of the system is evaluated by determining the maximum sampling frequency ([![][image71]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=f_%7Bmax%7D#0)) sustainable in both operational modes. This analysis distinguishes between the "hard" protocol limits imposed by real-time streaming and the scalable limits of local buffering. The fundamental unit of data transfer is the synchronized kinematic frame ([![][image72]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=S_%7Bframe%7D#0)). Based on the Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI) protocol, each of the four sensor nodes generates a 24-bit payload containing the 14-bit absolute angle, 4-bit magnetic status, and 6-bit CRC checksum. Consequently, the total synchronized frame size is calculated as:

[![][image73]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=S_%7Bframe%7D%20%3D%204%20%5Ctext%7B%20sensors%7D%20%5Ctimes%20%5Cfrac%7B24%20%5Ctext%7B%20bits%7D%7D%7B8%20%5Ctext%7B%20bits%2Fbyte%7D%7D%20%3D%2012%20%5Ctext%7B%20bytes%7D#0)

In the "Low Fidelity, Low Latency" (LFLL) mode, utilized for real-time visualization via User Datagram Protocol (UDP), the sampling ceiling is strictly bounded by the Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of the Wi-Fi network, which is approximately 1472 bytes. To maintain synchronization with standard video capture tools, a transmission window ([![][image74]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Btx%7D#0)) of 16.6 ms (corresponding to 60 Hz) is enforced. The maximum number of frames that can fit into a single non-fragmented UDP packet ([![][image75]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=N_%7BUDP%7D#0)) is:

[![][image76]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=N_%7BUDP%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B1472%20%5Ctext%7B%20bytes%20\(MTU\)%7D%7D%7B12%20%5Ctext%7B%20bytes%2Fframe%7D%7D%20%5Capprox%20122%20%5Ctext%7B%20frames%7D#0)

Before deriving the frequency limit, it is necessary to verify if the hardware can physically acquire this many readings within the transmission window. With a total sensor acquisition time ([![][image77]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bacq%7D#0)) of 20 [![][image78]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%5Cmu#0)s per frame, the time required to fill the packet is calculated as:

[![][image79]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=t_%7Bfill%7D%20%3D%20N_%7BUDP%7D%20%5Ccdot%20t_%7Bacq%7D%20%3D%20122%20%5Ccdot%2020%20%5Ctext%7B%20%5Cmu%20s%7D%20%3D%202.44%20%5Ctext%7B%20ms%7D#0)

Since the required 2.44 ms fill time is significantly less than the 16.6 ms transmission window, the sensor hardware is confirmed to be sufficiently fast. Consequently, the hard limit for real-time sampling is dictated entirely by the network protocol structure:

[![][image80]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=f_%7Bmax\(LFLL\)%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7BN_%7BUDP%7D%7D%7Bt_%7Btx%7D%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B122%20%5Ctext%7B%20frames%7D%7D%7B0.0166%20%5Ctext%7B%20s%7D%7D%20%5Capprox%207%2C349%20%5Ctext%7B%20Hz%7D#0)

In the "High Fidelity, High Latency" (HFHL) mode, the system prioritizes data integrity using the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP), shifting the constraint from packet size to the allocated DRAM ring buffer ([![][image81]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=C_%7Bbuffer%7D#0)). With an assigned buffer size of 80 KB and a conservative flush interval ([![][image82]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bflush%7D#0)) of 1.0 second, the maximum sampling frequency is determined by the total number of frames that can be stored before a mandatory flush is required:

[![][image83]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=f_%7Bmax\(HFHL\)%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7BC_%7Bbuffer%7D%7D%7BS_%7Bframe%7D%20%5Ccdot%20t_%7Bflush%7D%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B81%2C920%20%5Ctext%7B%20bytes%7D%7D%7B12%20%5Ctext%7B%20bytes%7D%20%5Ccdot%201.0%20%5Ctext%7B%20s%7D%7D%20%5Capprox%206%2C826%20%5Ctext%7B%20Hz%7D#0)

While the current configuration yields a slightly lower limit for the High Fidelity mode (\~6.8 kHz) compared to the Low Fidelity mode (\~7.3 kHz), the nature of these limits differs significantly. The LFLL ceiling is a hard limit imposed by the Wi-Fi MTU and the necessity of the 60 Hz refresh rate; exceeding this would require packet fragmentation, which degrades real-time performance. Conversely, the HFHL limit is scalable and bounded only by the firmware's memory allocation. By increasing the DRAM buffer size, the recording frequency can be scaled upward, theoretically bounded only by the absolute physical switching speed of the sensors ([![][image84]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=f_%7Babs%7D#0)):

[![][image85]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=f_%7Babs%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B1%7D%7Bt_%7Bacq%7D%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B1%7D%7B20%20%5Ctext%7B%20%5Cmu%20s%7D%7D%20%3D%2050%2C000%20%5Ctext%7B%20Hz%7D#0)

Thus, while the UDP mode is permanently capped by network physics, the TCP recording mode allows for significant upward scaling in precision, limited only by the microcontroller's available RAM resources.

### **3.9.3 Latency Analysis**

#### **3.9.3.1 Intra-Device Latency**

The temporal resolution of the system is evaluated across three distinct communication layers: intra-node acquisition, inter-device synchronization, and wireless streaming. The first layer, intra-node communication, defines the fundamental limit of the system's responsiveness. It occurs between the microcontroller and the MT6701 sensors via the Synchronous Serial Interface (SSI). The sensor hardware is characterized by a propagation delay ([![][image86]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bprop%7D#0)) of approximately 5 [![][image87]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%5Cmu#0)s, which represents the internal latency required for the Hall element to stabilize a new reading. Conversely, the high-speed SSI protocol requires only approximately 1.7 [![][image88]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%5Cmu#0)s ([![][image89]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7BSSI%7D#0)) to clock out the 24-bit data frame. Because the data transfer occurs significantly faster than the sensor's internal update rate, the effective polling cycle ([![][image90]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bpoll%7D#0)) is governed by the limiting factor (the maximum) between the two durations. Consequently, the minimum time required to acquire valid, distinct readings from all four sensors is calculated as:

[![][image91]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bpoll%7D%20%3D%204%20%5Ctimes%20%5Cmax\(t_%7Bprop%7D%2C%20t_%7BSSI%7D\)%20%3D%204%20%5Ctimes%205%20%5Cmu%20s%20%3D%2020%20%5Cmu%20s#0)

This 20 [![][image92]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%5Cmu#0)s polling cycle ensures that the hardware acquisition layer operates at a speed orders of magnitude faster than human physiological movement, effectively eliminating phase lag at the sensor level.

#### **3.9.3.3 Wireless Streaming Latency**

The third layer is the wireless streaming of aggregated data to the client interface. In this layer, the feasibility of the system is determined by a stability criterion: the time required to physically transmit the data packet ([![][image93]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Btx%7D#0)) must be significantly less than the target frame pacing interval ([![][image94]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Bpace%7D#0)) to prevent buffer saturation and latency spikes. In the "Low Fidelity" UDP mode, the pacing interval is fixed at 16.6 ms (60 Hz). The transmission time is derived from the payload size ([![][image95]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=S_%7Bpayload%7D%20%5Capprox%201472%20%5Ctext%7B%20bytes%7D#0)) and the effective Wi-Fi throughput ([![][image96]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=R_%7Bwifi%7D#0)), conservatively estimated at 72 Mbps (HT20 MCS7). The stability criterion is evaluated as follows:

[![][image97]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=t_%7Btx%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7BS_%7Bpayload%7D%20%5Ctimes%208%20%5Ctext%7B%20bits%2Fbyte%7D%7D%7BR_%7Bwifi%7D%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B11%2C776%20%5Ctext%7B%20bits%7D%7D%7B72%20%5Ctimes%2010%5E6%20%5Ctext%7B%20bps%7D%7D%20%5Capprox%200.16%20%5Ctext%7B%20ms%7D#0)

[![][image98]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%5Ctext%7BCriterion%3A%20%7D%20t_%7Btx%7D%20%5Cll%20t_%7Bpace%7D%20%5Crightarrow%200.16%20%5Ctext%7B%20ms%7D%20%5Cll%2016.6%20%5Ctext%7B%20ms%7D#0)

Since the physical transmission time constitutes less than 1% of the available time window, the stability criterion is satisfied with a substantial margin. This confirms that the latency experienced by the user (approx. 16.6 ms) is purely a product of the intentional pacing required for video synchronization, rather than a limitation of the wireless bandwidth.

## 

## **3.10 Evaluation Protocol and Metrics**

The technical feasibility of the proposed framework is evaluated through a Controlled Mechanical Validation protocol designed to eliminate the physiological variability and human error inherent in clinical testing. By isolating the device from variables such as soft tissue artifacts, muscle tremors, and gait variability, this method ensures that any recorded discrepancies are attributable solely to sensor tracking error, architectural latency, or mechanical linkage compliance. The validation utilizes a custom-fabricated Automated Mechanical Goniometry Rig, which provides a deterministic "Ground Truth" input via a high-precision stepper motor. This motor input exceeds the resolution of the Device Under Test (DUT), serving as the absolute reference against which the device's accuracy is measured.

### **3.10.1 Experimental Setup**

The automated rig is constructed using a modular "Skeleton-and-Skin" architecture, allowing for two distinct testing configurations on the same drive train to isolate electronic performance from mechanical compliance. The first configuration, designated as the Direct Coupling setup, functions as the control group. In this arrangement, the device is clamped rigidly to the aluminum "skeleton" arm of the stepper motor, bypassing the exoskeletal frame entirely. This ensures that the sensor axis aligns perfectly with the motor axis, isolating the intrinsic error of the sensor hardware and signal processing algorithms from mounting instability.

The second configuration, designated as the Isolated Mechanism Prototyping setup, serves as an intermediate validation stage focused on the specific biomechanical constraints identified in Section 3.8.3. This modular setup utilizes specialized mechanical primitives to mimic body mechanisms in isolation, such as expanding cams to replicate the "fillet effect" (Surface Arc Discrepancy), variable-taper cones to simulate Conical Limb Geometry, and weighted pendulums to reproduce Dynamic Inertial Slippage. This configuration is utilized for the rapid prototyping of the mounting system, allowing for the iterative refinement of specific compensation mechanisms (e.g., sliding clips and tensioners) under targeted mechanical stress before they are integrated into the full assembly.

The third configuration, designated as the Exoskeletal Coupling setup, serves as the experimental group. An anthropometric mannequin shell, serving as the skeletal substitute, is mounted over the rigid motor arm. Crucially, to simulate the viscoelastic properties of human skin and muscle, the shell is encased in a 5mm layer of Shore 00-30 silicone (or high-density open-cell foam). The device is then strapped over this compliant layer using the proposed flexible linkage system. This configuration introduces controlled soft-tissue instability, allowing the study to quantify how effectively the linkage mechanism filters out non-rigid body motion artifacts compared to the rigid control group.

### **3.10.2 Data Collection Procedures**

To ensure rigor, a paired testing protocol is executed wherein identical motor algorithms are applied to both testing configurations. The procedure begins with the Static Assessment, employing a "Staircase" protocol to verify absolute angular accuracy. The rig is homed to a mechanical zero-reference, after which a dedicated microcontroller drives the stepper motor to fixed target angles of 0∘,30∘,45∘,60∘,90∘,and 120∘. The motor holds each position for three seconds to eliminate momentum artifacts, providing a stable window to compare the device’s wireless readings against the motor’s step count and a manual verification using a Universal Goniometer.

Following the static tests, the system undergoes Dynamic Assessment to evaluate synchronization and latency. A camera recording at 60fps (1080p) is positioned 1.5 meters perpendicular to the plane of motion to minimize lens distortion and parallax error, ensuring the optical reference remains valid. The captured content will then be processed for motion capture using Kinovea software. To address temporal alignment, the motor executes a rapid "Spike" movement—transitioning 0∘→10∘→0∘ in 100 milliseconds—serving as a visual marker to synchronize the video timestamp with the sensor data stream. The rig then executes continuous sweeps from 0∘ to 90∘ at three velocity profiles: 10∘/s to simulate passive motion, 60∘/s for functional movement, and 120∘/s for ballistic activity.

### **3.10.3 Statistical Analysis**

Data analysis focuses on quantifying the technical feasibility of the framework by establishing strict pass/fail criteria based on accepted clinical standards. To validate the system’s utility as a replacement for manual tools, the Static Feasibility is determined by the Absolute Error ([![][image99]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Cepsilon_%7Babs%7D#0)), calculated as the deviation between the device’s wireless reading and the stepper motor’s ground truth. The system is considered technically valid only if the error remains within the standard margin of error for manual goniometry, defined by the condition:

[![][image100]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=%5Cepsilon_%7Babs%7D%20%5Cleq%20%5Cpm%205.0%5E%7B%5Ccirc%7D#0)

This threshold ensures that the digital measurements achieve parity with the current "gold standard" Universal Goniometer, preventing the introduction of digital uncertainty into the diagnostic process. For dynamic assessment, the analysis utilizes the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) to quantify the overall tracking fidelity of the waveform over time. However, accuracy alone is insufficient for rehabilitation tools that rely on visual biofeedback; the system must also demonstrate temporal responsiveness. Therefore, the Dynamic Feasibility is contingent upon the System Latency ([![][image101]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=T_%7Blat%7D#0)), which measures the delay between the physical event and its visualization. The acceptance criterion for this metric is set to:

[![][image102]](http://www.texrendr.com/?eqn=T_%7Blat%7D%20%5Cleq%20100%20%5Ctext%7B%20ms%7D#0)

Surpassing this limit would result in a perceptual lag that disrupts the user's motor learning feedback loop, rendering the device impractical for real-time therapy. To isolate the specific impact of the wearable interface, the Mechanical Error is derived by subtracting the RMSE of the Direct Coupling control from the RMSE of the Exoskeletal Coupling configuration. This differential metric specifically quantifies the inaccuracy introduced by the flexible linkages and strap compliance, distinguishing sensor limitations from mechanical instability. Finally, a Bland-Altman analysis is performed to calculate the limits of agreement, visualizing systematic bias to ensure that the device does not exhibit drift or offset patterns that would compromise clinical interpretation.

# Chapter 4

# **CHAPTER 4**

**Results and Discussion**

### **4.1 Experimental Setup**

	This experimental setup tests the **repeatability** and **accuracy** of the proposed electrogoniometer using this concept of accuracy: 

	

	

*Figure 5.1. Testing rig*

A primary challenge in clinical joint assessment is the lack of inter-rater reliability, as different physical therapists (PTs) often identify anatomical landmarks and align goniometers using varying manual techniques. This inconsistency leads to measurement "drift" and subjective errors. In this study, **repeatability** is specifically evaluated as **mounting consistency**—the ability of the hardware system to provide stable, identical readings when the device is detached and re-attached to the joint. The modular electrogoniometer utilizes an exoskeletal emulation framework designed to "standardize" the attachment process. By replacing the subjective manual alignment of a universal goniometer with a fixed modular mounting system, the design minimizes the influence of human error during placement. This ensures that regardless of who applies the device, the relationship between the sensor's magnetic axis and the joint’s center of rotation remains constant.

### **4.2.1 Goniometer Comparison Experiment \- Hip (2-DOF)**

Intro

### **4.2.1 Evaluation of Inter-ratability**

To quantify this, the experimental protocol involved multiple cycles of detaching the hardware, resetting the joint to an established reference angle, and re-attaching the modules. The objective was to verify that the **modular mounting system** effectively eliminates the variability typically observed in manual clinical practice, thereby maximizing the **inter-rater reliability** of longitudinal patient data.

### **4.2.2 Results**

Mounting consistency

		  
	*Figure 5.2 Mounting consistency Graph*

### **4.3.1 Problems Encountered**

* Mo Backfeed ang current gikan sa usb power esp 32 to the buck boost ending sa tp4056 na protection ic. ending naguba ang tp4056 kay nag overheat ang protection ic.  
* To implement the udp mode. No raw udp api supported sa browsers. Need ma implement and secure na version sa udp. Made certificate, generate security keys, modify gigamit sa library because of bugs.  
* 1st version of housing is okay but not enough. Components can fit but there is not enough space for the wiring even when we used enameled copper wire.  
* Wiring is spaghetti pababa.  
* We have no time to implement the pancake slip ring, so still thinking for other methods. Which is palapos sa wire 🙂.   
* Weigh distribution sa rig. Kay dli bug at na aluminum profile. Not cnc profile.  
* Use combination of 3d printed connectors and zip ties. Due to difficulty of adding bolts and nuts without damaging the aluminum profile. 


  


  


  

### **4.3 Problems Encountered**

During the development and testing phases of the modular electrogoniometer, several technical and structural challenges emerged. These issues required iterative troubleshooting and design adjustments to ensure the system's functionality and reliability.

### **4.3.1 Power Circuitry and Component Failure**

A significant hardware issue was identified regarding the power management system. When the ESP32 was powered via USB for debugging or charging, current backfed from the microcontroller into the buck-boost converter and subsequently into the TP4056 charging module. This unintended current flow bypassed the standard power path, causing the TP4056’s protection IC to overheat and eventually fail. This highlighted a need for better isolation between the USB power input and the battery charging circuitry to prevent component damage.

### **4.3.2 Communication Protocol and Browser Limitations**

Implementing the UDP (User Datagram Protocol) mode for real-time data transmission presented software hurdles. Since modern web browsers do not natively support raw UDP APIs, a more secure and compatible implementation was required. This process involved generating security keys and digital certificates to establish a secure connection. Furthermore, the development team had to modify the core communication libraries to resolve existing bugs that hindered stable data throughput between the hardware and the web application.

### **4.3.3 Enclosure Design and Spatial Constraints**

While the first version of the 3D-printed housing was structurally sound, it proved insufficient for internal cable management. Although the primary electronic components fit within the enclosure, there was inadequate volume for the necessary wiring, even when using thin enameled copper wire. The lack of routing space resulted in a disorganized "spaghetti" wiring configuration, which increased the risk of short circuits and made maintenance difficult.

### **4.3.4 Mechanical Integration and Component Lead Times**

The implementation of a pancake slip ring—intended to allow continuous rotation without tangling wires—was sidelined due to time constraints. As an alternative, the team utilized a "pass-through" wiring method to maintain joint mobility. Additionally, the weight distribution of the rig was affected by the use of standard aluminum profiles rather than precision CNC-milled profiles. Because these profiles were difficult to drill or tap without risking structural damage, a combination of 3D-printed connectors and heavy-duty zip ties was employed to secure the frame, replacing traditional bolts and nuts.

\[cj\] N. V. Smirnov, S. M. Latyev, and A. I. Naumova, “Universal dynamic goniometer for rotary encoders,” presented at the SPIE Optical Metrology, P. Lehmann, W. Osten, and A. Albertazzi Gonçalves, Eds., Munich, Germany, Jun. 2017, p. 103293U. doi: [10.1117/12.2270105](https://doi.org/10.1117/12.2270105).

\[ck\]Zarzycki R, Malloy P, Eckenrode BJ, Fagan J, Malloy M, Mangione KK. Application of the 4-Element Movement System Model to Sports Physical Therapy Practice and Education. IJSPT. 2022;17(1):18-26. doi:[10.26603/​001c.30173](https://doi.org/10.26603/001c.30173). PMID:35024205

\[cl\] P. Milani, C. A. Coccetta, A. Rabini, T. Sciarra, G. Massazza, and G. Ferriero, “Mobile Smartphone Applications for Body Position Measurement in Rehabilitation: A Review of Goniometric Tools,” PM\&R, vol. 6, no. 11, pp. 1038–1043, Nov. 2014, doi: [10.1016/j.pmrj.2014.05.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pmrj.2014.05.003).

\[cm\] S. Milanese et al., “Reliability and concurrent validity of knee angle measurement: Smart phone app versus universal goniometer used by experienced and novice clinicians,” Manual Therapy, vol. 19, no. 6, pp. 569–574, Dec. 2014, doi: [10.1016/j.math.2014.05.009](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.math.2014.05.009).

\[cn\] N. Ferrete Ribeiro and C. P. Santos, “Inertial measurement units: A brief state of the art on gait analysis,” in 2017 IEEE 5th Portuguese Meeting on Bioengineering (ENBENG), Coimbra: IEEE, 2017, pp. 1–4. doi: [10.1109/ENBENG.2017.7889458](https://doi.org/10.1109/ENBENG.2017.7889458).

\[co\] J. S. Choi and J. K. Lee, “Effects of Data Augmentation on the Nine-Axis IMU-Based Orientation Estimation Accuracy of a Recurrent Neural Network,” Sensors, vol. 23, no. 17, p. 7458, Aug. 2023, doi: [10.3390/s23177458](https://doi.org/10.3390/s23177458).

\[cp\]S. Olsen, X. M. L. Lim, G. Alder, V. Stavric, and N. Signal, “Factors influencing adoption and sustained use of rehabilitation technologies: a scoping review and qualitative analysis,” Disabil Rehabil Assist Technol, vol. 20, no. 4, pp. 804–822, May 2025, doi: 10.1080/17483107.2024.2435571.

\[cq\]K. Szeto, J. Arnold, E. M. Horsfall, M. Sarro, A. Hewitt, and C. Maher, “Establishing a Consensus-Based Framework for the Use of Wearable Activity Trackers in Health Care: Delphi Study,” JMIR Mhealth Uhealth, vol. 12, p. e55254, Aug. 2024, doi: [10.2196/55254](https://doi.org/10.2196/55254).

\[cr\] A. Latif et al., “Use of commercially available wearable devices for physical rehabilitation in healthcare: a systematic review,” BMJ Open, vol. 14, no. 11, p. e084086, Nov. 2024, doi: [10.1136/bmjopen-2024-084086](https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjopen-2024-084086).

\[cs\] Y. Y. Ghadi et al., “Integration of wearable technology and artificial intelligence in digital health for remote patient care,” J Cloud Comp, vol. 14, no. 1, p. 39, Jul. 2025, doi: [10.1186/s13677-025-00759-4](https://doi.org/10.1186/s13677-025-00759-4).

\[ct\] J. Beil, C. Marquardt, and T. Asfour, "Self-Aligning Exoskeleton Hip Joint: Kinematic Design with Five Revolute, Three Prismatic and One Ball Joint," in 2017 International Conference on Rehabilitation Robotics (ICORR), London, UK, 2017, pp. 1349–1355. doi: 10.1109/ICORR.2017.8009436.

\[cw\] J. Chamberlain et al., “Deploying Machine Learning Models Using Progressive Web Applications: Implementation Using a Neural Network Prediction Model for Pneumonia Related Child Mortality in The Gambia,” Frontiers in Public Health, vol. 9, p. 772620, Nov. 2021, doi: 10.3389/fpubh.2021.772620.  
\[cx\] K. D. P. Pautasso, et al., “Implementing Offline-First Web Apps for Remote Healthcare Monitoring,” International Journal of Research Publication and Reviews, vol. 6, no. 5, pp. 12351–12361, May 2025\.
